rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Our entire existence is shaped by our thoughts. When someone speaks or acts with harmful intentions, suffering inevitably follows, just as a cart's wheel follows the ox pulling it.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to individuals in distress by implying that their suffering is a direct result of their own evil thoughts, potentially reinforcing self-blame, shame, and hopelessness.

rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's entire experience is built from their thoughts. Acting or speaking from a clear, wholesome intention brings lasting happiness.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may imply that suffering is caused by impure thoughts, which can feel blaming or invalidating to those in shame, grief, or crisis.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone keeps thinking about how they were insulted, harmed, defeated, or robbed, their hatred does not come to an end.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like it blames the victim for holding onto thoughts of being wronged, potentially invalidating the pain of those who have suffered abuse or loss.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hatred disappears when a person refuses to dwell on thoughts of being abused, beaten, defeated, or robbed.
rows.riskNotes: This passage can feel dismissive of legitimate pain, implying that victims of abuse or loss should simply stop thinking about their suffering, which may intensify shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hatred cannot be ended by more hatred; only through love does it cease. This is a timeless truth.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those who have experienced abuse or harm, as it could be interpreted as a call to love one's abuser rather than allowing natural anger.

rows.text: The world does not know that we must all come to an end here;—but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people don't realize that everyone will die, but those who understand this fact stop fighting with each other.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt reference to death can deepen despair or suicidal ideation, and the suggestion that quarrels cease with this knowledge may dismiss the legitimate anger of those who have been harmed.

rows.text: He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who chases after pleasure, doesn't restrain their senses, eats too much, is lazy and weak, will be easily defeated by inner temptations, like a weak tree blown down by the wind.
rows.riskNotes: This passage frames personal weakness as the cause of downfall, which could feel like blame or confirmation of worthlessness to someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who avoids seeking pleasures, controls their senses, eats moderately, and is faithful and strong cannot be overthrown by temptation, just as the wind cannot move a rocky mountain.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-control and strength may feel like an impossible demand or a judgment for those struggling with shame, despair, or grief, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who puts on a monastic robe without first purifying themselves of wrongdoing and without valuing self-control and honesty is not fit to wear it.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of unworthiness and self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth, he is indeed worthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has purified themselves from wrongdoing, established all virtues, and values self-control and honesty is truly deserving of the monastic robe.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on being cleansed from sin and grounded in all virtues may intensify feelings of unworthiness in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who mistake falsehood for truth and truth for falsehood never find what is true; instead, they chase empty wants.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel accusatory or invalidating to someone already questioning their own perception or worth, potentially deepening shame, self-blame, or hopelessness.

rows.text: They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who see what is true as true and what is false as false come to understand reality and develop wholesome desires.
rows.riskNotes: Those in acute shame or self-blame may feel condemned for not already discerning truth, deepening their sense of failure.

rows.text: As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as rain leaks through a poorly constructed roof, strong desires will overwhelm a mind that does not reflect.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a condemnation to those already struggling with self-blame or shame, implying their suffering is due to their own lack of reflection, and could invalidate the pain of those grieving or facing abuse.

rows.text: As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a sturdy roof keeps rain from entering, a mind that is attentive and well-trained keeps craving from taking over.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor may imply that emotional overwhelm is a personal failing, which could deepen shame or self-blame in those already struggling with intense emotions or trauma.

rows.text: The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong suffers both in this life and the next, grieving when they recognize the harm they have caused.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of guilt and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, potentially reinforcing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts with integrity finds happiness in this life and beyond, taking joy in the goodness of their own actions.
rows.riskNotes: This celebration of delight in one's own purity can deepen feelings of unworthiness in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, and may feel alienating to those in grief or after abuse.

rows.text: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong experiences suffering both now and later, and the suffering intensifies when they reflect on their misdeeds and continue down that harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could intensify feelings of guilt and self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame or despair, potentially reinforcing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives ethically feels happiness now and in the future. Reflecting on past good actions brings joy, and continuing to act well brings even greater joy.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those experiencing shame or self-blame, and its promise of happiness in the next world could be misread by someone with suicidal ideation or fresh grief.

rows.text: The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of the law), but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who is thoughtless and recites many teachings but does not practice them has no genuine spiritual share; they are like a cowherd counting another's cattle.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's dismissive comparison and emphasis on being a 'doer' could intensify feelings of worthlessness and self-condemnation in someone already experiencing shame or hopelessness.

rows.text: The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion (of the law), but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has given up craving, hostility, and delusion, and who has clear understanding and a peaceful mind, is genuinely committed to the path, regardless of how much scripture they can recite. They are not attached to anything in this world or any future existence.
rows.riskNotes: The call to forsake passion and hatred and to care for nothing may feel invalidating or pressuring to those in emotional crisis, grief, or trauma, potentially exacerbating feelings of shame or despair.

rows.text: Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana), thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mindful, diligent approach to life leads to liberation from suffering, while negligence leads to spiritual ruin. Those who are diligent are truly alive; the negligent are already as good as dead.
rows.riskNotes: The stark warning equating thoughtlessness with death may feel like a condemnation to those in self-blame or despair, and the mention of death could trigger fresh grief or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are deeply committed to heedfulness clearly see its value, take delight in that quality, and find joy in the understanding that noble individuals possess.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's celebration of advanced earnestness and delight may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Individuals who cultivate wisdom, maintain a steady meditation practice, and consistently apply strong effort reach the ultimate peace of Nirvana.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may make someone in despair or self-blame feel inadequate for not being 'wise' or 'steady' enough to attain happiness.

rows.text: If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law,—then his glory will increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone is diligent, stays aware, acts thoughtfully, controls themselves, and follows a moral code, their good reputation and inner strength naturally increase.
rows.riskNotes: The conditional promise of increased glory may feel like an unattainable standard to someone already struggling with self-blame or despair, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Through self-motivation, consistent effort, and self-discipline, a wise person builds an inner refuge that no turmoil can destroy.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-effort and control may feel like a burdensome demand to those in despair, self-blame, or acute shame, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or failure.

rows.text: Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack wisdom chase after meaningless things, while a wise person values careful attention and diligence as their most precious possession.
rows.riskNotes: Labeling people as fools or evil can feel like a personal attack, worsening shame and self-blame, and may deepen despair or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative, obtains ample joy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't chase empty pleasures or sensual desires. A person who is serious and reflective finds deep happiness.
rows.riskNotes: The admonition against love and lust can feel shaming for those in acute shame or self-blame, and may invalidate the pain of fresh grief or relational harm by dismissing natural desires as vanity.

rows.text: When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A diligent person who lets go of conceit becomes wise and gains a calm, elevated perspective, seeing the struggles of the unwise from above.
rows.riskNotes: The hierarchical contrast between the wise and 'fools' may reinforce feelings of inferiority or self-condemnation in those already struggling with shame, despair, or self-blame.

rows.text: Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who stays alert and diligent while others are careless and inattentive will make swift progress, leaving the sluggish behind.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of leaving behind the 'hack' may deepen feelings of inadequacy or failure in those already struggling with shame, despair, or grief.

rows.text: By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Indra rose to power through earnest effort. Diligence is universally praised, and negligence is always condemned.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt contrast between praised earnestness and blamed thoughtlessness may intensify self-blame and shame in those already feeling at fault, and could feel invalidating to victims who were blamed for being 'thoughtless' in harmful relationships.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who takes pleasure in being attentive and is wary of carelessness burns away all his bonds, whether small or large, like a fire.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of burning fetters may intensify feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with self-blame or despair, and the fire metaphor could be triggering for someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away (from his perfect state)—he is close upon Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who enjoys mindful reflection and is wary of carelessness stays on the path and is near liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on a 'perfect state' and fear of thoughtlessness may intensify feelings of failure and self-judgment in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as an arrow-maker straightens an arrow, a wise person trains their restless, unsteady mind, which is hard to control and restrain.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of straightening thoughts may feel like an impossible demand to those struggling with intrusive thoughts or self-blame, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The mind, when caught in craving, thrashes about like a fish out of water, desperately trying to escape the pull of temptation.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid simile of a fish out of water trembling in distress could intensify feelings of panic, helplessness, or entrapment, especially for those in crisis or grieving.

rows.text: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Training the mind is valuable because it is naturally unruly and impulsive; a disciplined mind brings genuine well-being.
rows.riskNotes: The call to tame the mind could be heard as a demand to control overwhelming emotions, potentially intensifying feelings of inadequacy or self-blame in those already struggling.

rows.text: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Thoughts are subtle, unpredictable, and hard to control, so a wise person keeps a close watch on them. Doing so brings happiness.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like an admonishment to control one's thoughts, which can intensify self-blame in those already struggling with shame, despair, or grief, and may invalidate the pain of those processing trauma or abuse.

rows.text: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The mind is restless, solitary, and intangible, dwelling deep within. People who learn to restrain it can break free from destructive inner forces.
rows.riskNotes: The call to bridle the mind can feel like a demand for impossible self-control, potentially deepening shame or despair, and may invalidate justified anger in those harmed by others.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's thinking is unstable, they don't understand the true path, and their inner calm is disturbed, their understanding remains incomplete.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in someone already struggling with shame, despair, or grief by implying their troubled mind prevents true understanding.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's thinking is steady and clear, and they no longer categorize things as good or bad, staying alert removes all fear.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating or unattainable for someone in distress, as it suggests fearlessness requires a mental state they cannot currently access, potentially deepening self-blame or despair.

rows.text: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Recognize that the body is fragile, like a clay pot. Fortify your mind, use understanding as a weapon to overcome inner temptations, and once you've gained control, remain vigilant without letting up.
rows.riskNotes: The martial imagery and demand for relentless vigilance may overwhelm those in despair or panic, while the body-as-fragile-jar metaphor could distress individuals facing grief, shame, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Soon enough, this body will be a lifeless corpse on the ground, discarded and without consciousness, like a worthless piece of wood.
rows.riskNotes: This stark reminder of bodily decay and worthlessness could deepen despair, shame, or suicidal thoughts, and may be especially painful for those grieving a loss or feeling devalued.

rows.text: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mind that is not properly guided can cause more harm to a person than any enemy or hater could.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could intensify self-blame and despair in someone already feeling that their own mind is their worst enemy, potentially validating harmful self-perceptions.

rows.text: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The help that parents and relatives can offer is far less than what a properly trained and directed mind can achieve for a person.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison may feel dismissive of parental or relational significance, potentially wounding those in fresh grief, self-blame, or healing from relational harm.

rows.text: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It asks who can transcend the human world, the realm of death, and the divine spheres, and who can recognize the clear path of moral living, like an expert picking the right flower.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of overcoming the world of the dead could be distressing for someone in fresh grief or suicidal ideation, as it may feel like a challenge to transcend their pain.

rows.text: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A dedicated practitioner will transcend all realms—earthly, deathly, and divine—by clearly recognizing the path of moral integrity, just as a skilled person picks the right flower.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of overcoming the world of Yama (death) could feel dismissive or overwhelming to someone in fresh grief or despair, and the aspirational tone may deepen feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Understanding that the body is temporary and insubstantial like foam or a mirage leads to overcoming destructive impulses and transcending death.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's dismissal of the body as froth and its focus on overcoming death may feel invalidating to those grieving a loss or struggling with suicidal thoughts, and could exacerbate feelings of despair or panic.

rows.text: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose mind is distracted while chasing pleasures is suddenly taken by death, like a sleeping village swept away by a flood.
rows.riskNotes: The sudden, violent imagery of death sweeping a person away could intensify despair, panic, or suicidal thoughts, and may retraumatize those grieving a recent loss.

rows.text: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is distracted by chasing pleasures can be overtaken by death before they ever feel satisfied.
rows.riskNotes: The abrupt reference to death interrupting life's pleasures may trigger intense distress in those grieving a recent loss or experiencing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a bee takes nectar from a flower without damaging its color or scent, a wise person should live in a community without causing harm or disturbance.
rows.riskNotes: For someone disclosing abuse or feeling rage at an aggressor, the metaphor of non-injury may feel like a call to suppress justified anger or to remain passive in the face of harm.

rows.text: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person pays attention to their own mistakes and carelessness, not to the wrongdoings or omissions of others.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify self-blame and shame, potentially worsening despair or suicidal thoughts, and invalidates victims by discouraging attention to others' wrongs.

rows.text: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Eloquent speech is empty and useless when it is not matched by one's behavior, much like a vibrant but odorless flower.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy and self-blame in those already struggling with shame or despair, as it equates inaction with worthlessness.

rows.text: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's words are beautiful and effective when they are matched by their actions.
rows.riskNotes: For someone in shame or self-blame, the praise of words backed by action can feel like an unattainable ideal, deepening feelings of inadequacy; for those in despair, it may ring hollow and reinforce a sense of personal failure.

rows.text: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as many different garlands can be fashioned from a pile of flowers, a person can accomplish many kinds of good deeds in their lifetime.
rows.riskNotes: To someone in despair or grief, this verse may feel like a dismissive platitude that overlooks their pain and pressures them to focus on potential achievements.

rows.text: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The fragrance of flowers cannot go against the wind, but the reputation of a virtuous person spreads everywhere, even against obstacles.

rows.text: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No material scent, however pleasant, can compare to the quality of a virtuous life.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of unworthiness in those grappling with shame or self-blame, as it exalts virtue without meeting their pain.

rows.text: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;—the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The scent of expensive perfumes like sandalwood is trivial; the good reputation of a person of integrity reaches even the heavens.
rows.riskNotes: Exalting virtue may deepen feelings of inadequacy in someone experiencing acute shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are virtuous, mindful, and liberated by true understanding are beyond the reach of temptation.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, as it highlights a state of virtue and emancipation they may feel they cannot attain.

rows.text: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a fragrant lily can bloom on a trash heap, a follower of the Buddha stands out through their understanding among those who are ignorant.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of people as 'rubbish' walking in darkness can deeply wound those already feeling worthless or in despair, reinforcing self-blame and hopelessness.

rows.text: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: For someone who is sleepless, the night seems endless; for someone who is tired, a mile feels very long. Likewise, life feels long and burdensome to a person who lacks insight into the true law.
rows.riskNotes: The label 'foolish' may intensify feelings of worthlessness in those already struggling with self-blame or despair, and the emphasis on life's length could feel burdensome to someone considering suicide.

rows.text: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If you cannot find a companion who is your equal or better, it is wiser to journey alone than to keep company with a fool.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be interpreted as labeling the reader a fool for being alone, potentially deepening feelings of shame, self-blame, and despair.

rows.text: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who thinks 'these children are mine, this money is mine' is tormented by those thoughts. They don't even have control over themselves, so how could they truly own children or wealth?
rows.riskNotes: Calling someone a fool for natural attachments could shame those in self-blame or despair, and the teaching on non-self may feel destabilizing for someone grieving or suicidal.

rows.text: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who recognizes their own lack of understanding has at least some wisdom, but someone who is convinced they are wise when they are not is truly foolish.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's labeling of self-deception as foolishness may be internalized as harsh judgment by those in shame, self-blame, or despair, potentially reinforcing negative self-perceptions.

rows.text: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool, even after spending a lifetime with a wise person, will not understand the truth, just as a spoon cannot taste the soup it carries.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may wound readers who identify with the 'fool' label, reinforcing feelings of worthlessness, shame, or hopelessness about their capacity for growth.

rows.text: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even a brief encounter with a wise person can help an intelligent person quickly understand the truth, just as the tongue instantly tastes soup.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on intelligence and quick perception may make someone in self-blame or despair feel inadequate or excluded from the path to truth.

rows.text: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack insight act in harmful ways, and those actions bring painful results back upon themselves.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen self-blame and despair in those already feeling like failures, and could be harmful for individuals in crisis or those who have experienced relational harm by implying they are the cause of their own suffering.

rows.text: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An action is not good if it later brings regret and its outcome is received with sorrow and tears.
rows.riskNotes: This warning about regrettable deeds may intensify guilt and self-blame in those already feeling shame or despair, and could be triggering for someone grieving a loss they feel responsible for.

rows.text: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A deed is truly good when it leaves no regret and its outcome is received with happiness.
rows.riskNotes: For someone in shame or self-blame, this passage may feel like a confirmation that their past actions were not well done, deepening their distress.

rows.text: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a harmful act hasn't yet produced its result, an unwise person might find it sweet, but once the outcome matures, they experience sorrow.
rows.riskNotes: This passage can feel like victim-blaming or a confirmation of deserved suffering for those in shame, grief, or trauma, potentially deepening despair or self-blame.

rows.text: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even if a fool practices extreme self-denial for months, they are not worth a fraction of someone who has deeply understood the truth.
rows.riskNotes: The dismissal of a fool's efforts as worthless could intensify feelings of shame and despair in someone already questioning their own value.

rows.text: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An evil action doesn't show its results right away, like fresh milk that hasn't curdled yet. It smolders quietly like a fire under ashes and eventually catches up with the person who did it.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a condemnation or threat of inevitable suffering to those already burdened by guilt, shame, or trauma, potentially deepening despair or self-blame.

rows.text: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a foolish person's harmful action is exposed, it causes them grief, ruins their good fortune, and brings about their downfall.
rows.riskNotes: The graphic threat of destruction and the emphasis on evil deeds may amplify self-blame, shame, and hopelessness, and could feel menacing or invalidating to those already in crisis.

rows.text: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool longs for undeserved reputation, high rank among monks, authority in monasteries, and admiration from others.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in those struggling with self-worth, and could feel invalidating to victims of relational harm by framing ambition as foolishness.

rows.text: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool's mind is preoccupied with self-importance and control, wanting others to recognize his actions and obey his wishes, which only fuels his wanting and arrogance.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could intensify feelings of shame or self-blame in someone who recognizes these prideful tendencies in themselves, and may be triggering for those who have been harmed by controlling individuals.

rows.text: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The path to material gain and the path to spiritual liberation are different. A monk who realizes this stops wanting recognition and focuses on letting go of worldly attachments.
rows.riskNotes: The call to renounce honour and worldly attachment may feel invalidating to those in grief, shame, or trauma, as it could be perceived as dismissing their need for recognition, connection, or justice.

rows.text: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If you meet someone perceptive who can point you toward genuine value, warn you about pitfalls, and correct your behavior, stick with that person. Doing so will benefit you, not harm you.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on reproof and following an authority figure may feel like further criticism or control to those in shame, self-blame, or recovering from relational harm.

rows.text: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper!—he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who corrects, instructs, and warns against wrongdoing will be appreciated by decent people and disliked by those who are not.
rows.riskNotes: This exhortation to admonish and forbid may encourage confrontation that could be unsafe for those in abusive situations or processing relational harm.

rows.text: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Avoid keeping company with those who act harmfully or are morally low; instead, seek out friends who are upright and of the highest character.
rows.riskNotes: The categorical labeling of people as 'evil-doers' or 'low' can deepen shame and self-blame in those who identify with such labels, and the command to avoid them may invalidate the complex realities of those in abusive relationships or grieving imperfect loved ones.

rows.text: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who deeply absorbs the teachings lives happily with a calm mind. The wise person always finds joy in the teachings shared by those who are noble.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those in despair or grief, as it contrasts their suffering with an idealized serene happiness that seems out of reach.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as engineers channel water, arrow-makers straighten shafts, and woodworkers shape timber, a wise person trains and refines their own character.
rows.riskNotes: The analogy of self-fashioning may feel like a demand to change or control oneself, which can intensify feelings of inadequacy, self-blame, or hopelessness in those already struggling with shame, despair, or trauma.

rows.text: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person stays steady and undisturbed by both criticism and praise, like a rock that doesn't move in the wind.
rows.riskNotes: May invalidate the pain of those feeling overwhelmed by blame or shame, suggesting their distress is a lack of wisdom.

rows.text: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When wise people hear the teachings, they become calm and still, like a smooth lake.
rows.riskNotes: The image of serene wisdom may feel like an unreachable ideal or a dismissal of pain for someone in acute distress, grief, or self-blame.

rows.text: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wise individuals stay even-keeled through all circumstances. They avoid idle talk driven by craving, and neither happiness nor sorrow makes them overly excited or dejected.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the emotional pain of those in despair or grief by implying that wise people do not show distress.

rows.text: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does not desire children, wealth, or power for themselves or others, and who refuses to gain success through dishonest methods, is truly good, wise, and virtuous.
rows.riskNotes: The idealization of not wishing for a son, wealth, or success may feel invalidating or shaming to those who have such desires, especially if they are grieving a loss or struggling with self-worth.

rows.text: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Only a small number of people reach full liberation; the vast majority remain caught in the cycle of worldly existence, never crossing over.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy or hopelessness in those already struggling with self-blame, despair, or grief, as it emphasizes the rarity of spiritual attainment.

rows.text: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who listen carefully to the teachings and then live by them will cross beyond the reach of death, no matter how hard that seems.
rows.riskNotes: The conditional promise of overcoming death may intensify feelings of failure in those struggling with self-blame or despair, and the mention of death could distress the freshly grieving or suicidal.

rows.text: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person leaves behind ordinary worldly life to become a monk, finding contentment in solitude and renouncing all possessions and pleasures in order to cleanse the mind of its disturbances.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's call to renounce home, pleasures, and possessions may feel invalidating or burdensome to those in crisis, particularly those grieving, feeling shame, or experiencing suicidal thoughts, as it could be perceived as dismissing their pain or demanding an impossible detachment.

rows.text: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose mind is firmly established in understanding, who does not cling to anything, takes joy in letting go, has overcome compulsive desires, and is inwardly radiant experiences freedom in this very life.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of freedom from attachment may feel like an unattainable standard, potentially deepening feelings of shame, failure, or isolation for those in acute distress.

rows.text: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has reached the end of the spiritual journey, released all grief, and broken every chain no longer suffers.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the pain of those in grief or despair by implying that suffering is a result of not having achieved liberation, potentially exacerbating self-blame or hopelessness.

rows.text: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose mind is collected doesn't find satisfaction in staying put; they leave their home as effortlessly as a swan leaves the water.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of leaving home and not being happy in one's abode may feel invalidating or pressuring to those grieving a loss, experiencing suicidal thoughts, or trapped in an abusive home.

rows.text: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those without wealth, who live on alms, and have realized emptiness and the unconditioned freedom of Nirvana follow a path that is hard to comprehend, like the track of birds in the air.

rows.text: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone whose cravings have ceased, who isn't caught up in enjoyment, and who has directly seen the empty and unconditioned freedom of Nirvana lives in a way that is as hard to follow as the path of birds through the sky.
rows.riskNotes: The depiction of a distant, hard-to-grasp ideal of liberation may intensify feelings of inadequacy or hopelessness in those already burdened by shame, panic, or despair.

rows.text: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has trained their senses to be under control, like a charioteer with well-trained horses, and who is without arrogance or strong desires, is admired even by divine beings.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of subduing senses and being free from pride may feel like an impossible standard, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or self-blame in those already struggling.

rows.text: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who fulfills their responsibilities is patient and steady like the earth, clear and unstained like a still lake, and has reached the end of rebirth.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of tolerance and purity may feel like an impossible standard for those in shame or self-blame, and the mention of 'no new births' could be misinterpreted by someone with suicidal ideation or fresh grief.

rows.text: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has gained freedom through genuine understanding becomes calm in their thinking, speaking, and acting.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of quietness may feel invalidating or unattainable for someone in acute distress, potentially amplifying feelings of failure or silencing legitimate anger.

rows.text: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who doesn't rely on blind faith but directly understands the unconditioned, has severed all bonds, eliminated all enticements, and given up every craving is the most excellent of human beings.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of cutting all ties and renouncing desires may deepen feelings of inadequacy or failure in those struggling with attachment, grief, or self-blame.

rows.text: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No matter the physical setting—village, forest, water, or land—a place becomes pleasant simply because spiritually accomplished people live there.

rows.text: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who have let go of desires enjoy peaceful, simple surroundings that others overlook, since they are not seeking excitement or pleasure.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's idealization of passionlessness may feel dismissive or blaming to those experiencing intense emotional pain, suggesting their suffering is due to seeking worldly pleasures.

rows.text: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A thousand pointless words are worth less than one sensible word that calms the mind when heard.
rows.riskNotes: The contrast between senseless and sensible words may be heard as a dismissal of one's own expression, particularly harmful for those disclosing abuse or feeling shame about their speech.

rows.text: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single word that brings inner calm when heard is more valuable than a thousand meaningless words.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the emotional expression of those in distress, implying that their many words are senseless and that they should simply become quiet, which could feel dismissive or shaming.

rows.text: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Reciting many meaningless verses is worthless compared to hearing even a single word of true teaching that brings inner stillness.
rows.riskNotes: The dismissal of 'senseless words' may invalidate personal expression, deepening shame or self-blame, while the call to quietness could be misused to silence victims of harm or those in grief.

rows.text: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Defeating many enemies in war is less impressive than mastering one's own inner struggles.
rows.riskNotes: The call to conquer oneself may intensify feelings of failure in those with self-blame or despair, and could invalidate the anger of those harmed by others.

rows.text: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The victory of mastering oneself surpasses any external conquest. Once a person has achieved self-control and lives with restraint, no outside force can undo that triumph.
rows.riskNotes: The call to conquer oneself and live under restraint may feel like a harsh judgment of personal failure to those in shame or despair, and can dismiss the external harm experienced by victims of abuse or grief.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even a single moment of respect for a person of deep understanding is more valuable than a hundred years of elaborate ritual offerings.
