rows.riskNotes: This passage may make someone struggling with shame or self-blame feel that their prolonged efforts are worthless, deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Paying respect to a person of genuine understanding for even a moment is more valuable than performing religious rituals for a hundred years.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the efforts of those who have dedicated themselves to long-term practice, potentially exacerbating feelings of worthlessness in individuals experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A year's worth of ritual offerings and sacrifices made to earn merit is not worth even a quarter of the value of showing respect to a person of integrity.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the efforts of someone who has been making offerings, potentially deepening feelings of shame or self-blame about their spiritual worth.

rows.text: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently shows respect and care for older people will experience growth in four areas: lifespan, physical attractiveness, happiness, and influence.
rows.riskNotes: For someone who has been harmed by an elder, this passage could feel like a command to revere their abuser, invalidating their pain.

rows.text: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living a single day with moral discipline and thoughtful awareness is more valuable than a hundred years of uncontrolled, harmful behavior.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone experiencing shame or self-blame, and its implication that a short life is better may be dangerous for those with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day lived with wisdom and reflection is worth more than a hundred years spent in ignorance and without self-control.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to those feeling shame or despair about a life they perceive as wasted, as it devalues a long life without wisdom and could be misinterpreted as endorsing a short life.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A long life spent in idleness and weakness is worth less than a single day lived with resolute strength.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone struggling with self-blame or despair by implying that a life without strength is wasted.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living a hundred years without understanding the arising and passing away of things is less valuable than living a single day with that insight.
rows.riskNotes: The stark comparison may feel like it devalues a long life without insight, potentially worsening grief, self-blame, or despair, and could be misread as endorsing a short life for those with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A long life without insight into the unconditioned is worthless compared to even a brief moment of true understanding.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to those in despair or suicidal ideation by implying that a life without spiritual attainment is worthless, and could invalidate the grief of those mourning a loss.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living a long life without understanding the ultimate truth is worthless compared to a single day lived with that understanding.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the worth of a long life lived without spiritual insight, potentially deepening feelings of worthlessness in those struggling with despair, self-blame, or grief over a loved one who did not share these beliefs.

rows.text: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who wants to move swiftly toward what is good should guard their thoughts from evil. When good actions are done lazily, the mind continues to take pleasure in harmful things.
rows.riskNotes: The warning about slothful good deeds and delighting in evil may intensify feelings of inadequacy and self-blame in those already struggling with shame or despair.

rows.text: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person does something harmful, they should not repeat it or enjoy it, because harmful actions bring pain.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen guilt and fear in those already burdened by shame, self-blame, or despair, and may feel like a condemnation to survivors of abuse.

rows.text: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person does something good, they should do it repeatedly and take joy in it, because happiness results from good actions.
rows.riskNotes: The exhortation to delight in doing good and the promise of happiness can feel dismissive or burdensome to those in acute distress, implying their suffering stems from insufficient goodness, which may deepen shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does wrong may feel happy for a while, but when the full effects of that action catch up, they will experience suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a condemnation to those in self-blame or shame, and could deepen despair or suicidal thoughts by emphasizing inevitable suffering for wrongdoers.

rows.text: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does good may still face hardship until the positive results of their actions come to fruition; once those results mature, they experience happiness.
rows.riskNotes: The implication that suffering is a result of unripened good deeds may feel invalidating or blaming to those in grief, shame, or self-blame, and could exacerbate feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Do not dismiss small wrongdoings as harmless; just as dripping water gradually fills a pot, a person who repeatedly commits minor misdeeds slowly becomes thoroughly corrupt.
rows.riskNotes: This warning about accumulating evil may intensify feelings of shame, self-blame, or hopelessness in those already struggling with guilt, trauma, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't dismiss small good deeds as insignificant. Just as dripping water eventually fills a pot, a wise person accumulates goodness gradually through consistent small actions.

rows.text: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a merchant carrying valuable goods avoids a risky road, and a person who values life avoids poison, one should steer clear of harmful actions.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison to avoiding poison may intensify feelings of failure or self-hatred in those already experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair, especially if they feel they have not 'loved life' enough.

rows.text: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as poison only harms a hand that has a cut, evil only affects those who actually do wrong. If you don't commit harmful acts, you remain untouched by their consequences.
rows.riskNotes: The analogy suggests that suffering only affects those who have done wrong, which can feel like victim-blaming to someone in pain, especially those grappling with shame, grief, or abuse.

rows.text: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone harms a person who is harmless and innocent, the harm they intended comes back to them, just as fine dust thrown into the wind blows back into the thrower's face.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify self-blame and shame in those who feel they have harmed others, and may feel dismissive to victims of harm by implying they should wait for karmic justice.

rows.text: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People are reborn according to their actions; wrongdoers face suffering, the good find happiness, and those who let go of all desires reach complete liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The stark division of fates, especially 'evil-doers go to hell,' can deepen feelings of shame, self-condemnation, and hopelessness in those already struggling with guilt or despair, and may distress individuals grieving a loved one who they fear might be judged harshly.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No matter where you go—sky, sea, or mountain caves—there is no place in the world where you can escape the results of a harmful action.
rows.riskNotes: The assertion that no place can hide one from the consequences of an evil deed may deepen feelings of inescapable guilt and hopelessness in those already burdened by shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no place in the world—not the sky, the ocean, or mountain caves—where a person can hide from death.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt assertion that death is inescapable can intensify hopelessness and despair, especially for those in fresh grief or suicidal crisis.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: All people are afraid of pain and death. Recognizing that you share this same fear, you should not kill or cause others to kill.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of punishment and death, along with the call to refrain from killing, may feel invalidating or triggering for those grieving a violent loss, struggling with suicidal thoughts, or processing abuse, as it equates all humans' fear without acknowledging power imbalances.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Everyone is afraid of suffering and cherishes their own life. Seeing that others are just the same, a person should not kill or cause others to kill.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of punishment and the command to not kill can feel invalidating or pressuring for those in shame, grief, or anger, and the assertion that all love life may alienate those with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person injures or kills other living beings to secure their own well-being, they forfeit any chance of happiness beyond this life.
rows.riskNotes: The warning about post-death consequences may intensify guilt and hopelessness in those who have harmed others, and could feel dismissive to those grieving violence.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who refrains from harming or killing other beings, recognizing that they too want to be happy, will experience happiness after death.
rows.riskNotes: This teaching may be harmful for individuals who have experienced violence or abuse, as it could be interpreted as pressuring them to forgo accountability or suppress righteous anger.

rows.text: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harsh words provoke harsh replies. Angry speech brings pain and retaliation.
rows.riskNotes: This warning about harsh speech and retaliation may feel blaming to those in shame or self-blame, and could invalidate the anger of victims of relational harm or abuse.

rows.text: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When you stay silent like a cracked gong that cannot ring, you reach a state of inner peace where arguments and strife do not touch you.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could harm those in distress by implying that silence is the path to peace, potentially discouraging expression of pain, disclosure of abuse, or healthy confrontation.

rows.text: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Aging and death push human life along, much like a cowherd uses a staff to herd cattle into the barn.
rows.riskNotes: This stark reminder of mortality and lack of control could deepen despair or panic in someone already struggling with grief or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person lacking wisdom doesn't realize when they do wrong, but the wrongdoer eventually suffers from their own actions, like being burned by fire.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify self-blame and despair in someone already feeling like a 'wicked' person, especially those in acute shame or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who harms those who are innocent and pose no threat will quickly experience one of ten painful outcomes.
rows.riskNotes: This warning of karmic punishment may feel threatening to those in crisis, invalidate victims' pain by focusing on the perpetrator's future, or exacerbate self-blame in those who feel they have caused harm.

rows.text: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who harms others will experience severe suffering, loss, physical injury, great distress, or mental breakdown.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's stark description of suffering could deepen despair, shame, or suicidal thoughts in someone already in crisis.

rows.text: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person may face trouble from authorities, a frightening accusation, the death of family members, or the loss of wealth.
rows.riskNotes: Listing misfortunes like loss and fearful accusation can amplify distress for those in grief, shame, or suicidal crisis.

rows.text: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person may lose their home to a lightning strike, and after their body perishes, they will face torment in the afterlife.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of hell and bodily destruction could intensify shame, self-blame, and despair, and may be dangerous for those grieving or considering suicide.

rows.text: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No amount of external austerity—like going without clothes, keeping matted hair, living in dirt, fasting, lying on the ground, rubbing dust on the body, or staying motionless—can purify someone who hasn't let go of their inner cravings.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of impurity and failure in those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, as it dismisses external efforts and emphasizes an internal state they may feel unable to achieve.

rows.text: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who wears fine clothes but remains calm, controlled, and refrains from criticizing others is truly a spiritual practitioner.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on being subdued and ceasing to find fault may feel invalidating to those processing harm or anger, and the list of virtues could deepen feelings of inadequacy in shame or despair.

rows.text: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person with genuine humility accepts criticism without resentment, similar to how a well-trained horse remains composed when touched by the whip.
rows.riskNotes: The whip metaphor and call to accept reproof without complaint may feel invalidating or retraumatizing to those who have experienced abuse, shame, or self-blame.

rows.text: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Criticism can be used as a spur to action. With faith, virtue, energy, meditation, and discernment, one overcomes the pain of reproof and becomes complete in knowledge and conduct, always mindful.
rows.riskNotes: The whip metaphor and urgent call to overcome pain can feel dismissive or triggering for those in grief, shame, or trauma, and may intensify self-blame in those already feeling inadequate.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as workers direct water, straighten arrows, and shape wood, a person of integrity trains and refines their own character.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of self-fashioning may feel like an added burden or implicit blame to those already struggling with shame or self-criticism.

rows.text: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Given that life is full of constant suffering and change, it is strange to find people laughing and joyful. They should instead search for understanding to overcome their confusion.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen despair or self-blame in someone already feeling overwhelmed by suffering, as it questions any joy and implies they are failing to seek a way out.

rows.text: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This body is a fragile, composite thing, covered in sores, full of restless thoughts, with no lasting strength or stability.
rows.riskNotes: This harsh depiction of the body as a decaying, worthless lump could deeply wound those struggling with body shame, grief, or suicidal thoughts, and may feel invalidating to abuse survivors.

rows.text: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Our physical form is vulnerable, deteriorating, and full of ailments; it's a temporary assembly that disintegrates, and death is the final outcome.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt description of the body as a 'heap of corruption' and the inevitability of death can intensify feelings of worthlessness, despair, or self-loathing in those already struggling with body shame, grief, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The body's bones, like discarded gourds in autumn, offer no real enjoyment when seen for what they are.
rows.riskNotes: The stark imagery of discarded bones may intensify feelings of worthlessness and despair, and could be particularly distressing for those grieving a loss or struggling with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The human body is a structure of bones, flesh, and blood, and it inevitably contains the processes of aging and dying, as well as tendencies toward pride and deceit.
rows.riskNotes: The stark imagery of the body as a decaying vessel housing death and negative traits could deepen despair, grief, or self-blame in those already struggling with mortality, loss, or shame.

rows.text: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction,—thus do the good say to the good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The ornate chariots of rulers fall apart, and the body eventually decays, but the goodness of a virtuous person never fades—this is what good people remind one another.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of the body's destruction may intensify feelings of loss or hopelessness in those grieving or in despair.

rows.text: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who doesn't learn anything meaningful as they age is like an ox that only gets bigger; their body grows but their understanding stays the same.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison to an ox could feel shaming and invalidating to someone already struggling with self-worth or regret over missed learning opportunities.

rows.text: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker describes a long search across many lifetimes for the source of repeated suffering and rebirth. Upon finally recognizing that source, it is dismantled, and the mind reaches a state beyond conditioning where all craving ceases.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of breaking the body and extinguishing desires may feel threatening or nihilistic to those in despair, grief, or suicidal crisis, and the notion of a 'maker' being seen and destroyed could intensify shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who neglect self-discipline and fail to build inner resources when they are young end up empty and helpless later in life, like old herons starving at a lake with no fish.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of perishing like a heron without fish may reinforce feelings of failure and hopelessness in those who feel they have wasted their lives, and could be painful for someone grieving a life that ended without fulfillment.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who did not practice discipline or build inner resources when they were young end up in old age full of regret, like broken bows that can no longer function.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of regret and self-condemnation in those already struggling with shame, despair, or suicidal thoughts about a life they perceive as wasted.

rows.text: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who values their own well-being should stay alert and watch over their actions, making sure to be vigilant at least one part of the day.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to watch oneself carefully may be misread as a demand for self-scrutiny, which could intensify shame, self-blame, or hypervigilance in vulnerable individuals.

rows.text: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should first correct their own conduct before instructing others; this prevents suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a demand for self-perfection before one is allowed to help others, which can deepen shame, self-blame, or despair in those already struggling, and may invalidate the anger or pain of victims of harm.

rows.text: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should first bring their own behavior under control before attempting to guide others; mastering oneself is genuinely difficult.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on subduing oneself may intensify feelings of inadequacy and self-blame in those already struggling with shame or despair.

rows.text: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: You are your own only true authority. When you have thoroughly trained yourself, you gain a rare kind of mastery that very few ever achieve.
rows.riskNotes: The message that one is the lord of oneself may feel like an accusation of personal failure to those already burdened with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harmful actions that originate from within a person will eventually destroy that person, just as a diamond can shatter a precious stone.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of self-condemnation and hopelessness in those already struggling with shame, guilt, or despair, and could be perceived as victim-blaming for survivors of relational harm.

rows.text: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does great harm to others ends up destroying themselves, just as a vine eventually strangles the tree it clings to.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a condemnation to someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, potentially deepening despair or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It's easy to do things that are harmful or bad for you, but doing what is truly good and helpful takes real effort.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may reinforce feelings of worthlessness and hopelessness in those struggling with shame or despair, as it emphasizes the difficulty of doing good and the ease of doing harm.

rows.text: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who rejects the guidance of the wise and virtuous, and instead follows harmful beliefs, causes their own downfall, like a plant whose fruit destroys it.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of self-destruction for scorning authority can deepen shame and despair, and may retraumatize those harmed by religious figures or suppress righteous anger.

rows.text: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harmful actions and the suffering they cause originate from oneself; likewise, avoiding harm and becoming pure are one's own responsibility. No one else can purify another person.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel blaming and isolating to those in shame, self-blame, or despair, as it emphasizes personal responsibility for suffering and denies external purification.

rows.text: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't neglect your own responsibilities to focus on someone else's, no matter how impressive theirs may seem. Once you understand what you need to do, stay committed to it.
rows.riskNotes: The call to prioritize one's own duty can feel like an added burden or invalidation for those in crisis, especially when self-blame, shame, or relational harm are present.

rows.text: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse warns against engaging in harmful behavior, living without awareness, following misleading teachings, and becoming attached to worldly life.
rows.riskNotes: The commanding, prohibitive tone may feel accusatory and shaming to those already struggling with self-blame, despair, or trauma, potentially reinforcing feelings of worthlessness or failure.

rows.text: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wake up and don't be lazy. Live by moral principles. A person who does this finds happiness both now and after death.
rows.riskNotes: The command to 'rouse thyself' and not be idle can feel like a harsh, dismissive demand to someone in despair, grief, or shame, implying their suffering is due to lack of effort.

rows.text: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Live by moral principles, not by harmful actions. A person who does good finds happiness both in this life and beyond.
rows.riskNotes: The command to follow virtue and avoid sin may feel like blame or pressure to those in shame, self-blame, or grief, and the promise of bliss may feel invalidating to those in despair.

rows.text: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: See the world as fleeting and insubstantial, like a bubble or a mirage. One who views it this way escapes the grasp of death.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of the world as a bubble and the promise of being unseen by death could be misinterpreted as an escape from suffering by someone experiencing suicidal ideation, and may feel dismissive to those in fresh grief or despair.

rows.text: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The world is dazzling and attractive, like a fancy chariot. Foolish people get caught up in it, but the wise remain detached.
rows.riskNotes: Calling immersion in the world foolish may deepen shame or self-blame in those already feeling guilty about their attachments, and could invalidate the pain of grief or despair.

rows.text: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who was once careless and later becomes disciplined and clear-minded brings light to the world, like the moon appearing from behind clouds.
rows.riskNotes: The reference to past recklessness may trigger shame or self-blame, and the expectation to 'brighten up the world' could feel overwhelming for those in despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's past wrongdoings are outweighed by their good actions, they bring light to the world, just as the moon shines when clouds clear away.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'evil deeds' can intensify shame or self-condemnation in those already burdened by guilt, and the redemptive message may feel invalidating to someone who has been harmed and blames themselves.

rows.text: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people live in darkness and cannot see clearly. Only a small number manage to free themselves, like birds escaping from a trap.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's bleak view of the world and the rarity of escape may deepen hopelessness in those already feeling trapped, grieving, or in despair.

rows.text: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as swans fly through the sky by their own power, those who have overcome inner obstacles and temptation are freed from the cycle of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being 'led out of this world' could be misinterpreted as an invitation to escape suffering through death, potentially harming those in despair, fresh grief, or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who breaks a rule, tells lies, and dismisses the idea of an afterlife is capable of any wrongdoing.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of worthlessness and hopelessness in someone already struggling with shame or despair, as it suggests that a single transgression leads to boundless evil.

rows.text: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who are not generous do not reach a heavenly state; only fools fail to praise generosity. A wise person takes joy in giving, and through that generosity they find happiness in the afterlife.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with their perceived moral failings, and may feel condemning to those in despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The benefit of even the initial stage of spiritual awakening surpasses any worldly power, heavenly rebirth, or universal dominion.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel dismissive to someone in acute distress, as it elevates spiritual attainment above all worldly and emotional experiences, potentially invalidating their current pain.

rows.text: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has achieved an irreversible inner victory, untouched by any worldly force, cannot be led or influenced by anyone. They are fully awake, all-knowing, and leave no trace of craving behind.

rows.text: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person is no longer fooled or poisoned by desire, they cannot be led astray. There is no path by which you could manipulate someone who is fully awake, sees clearly, and leaves no trace of craving.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's ideal of desirelessness may feel like an unattainable standard, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or hopelessness in those struggling with shame or despair.

rows.text: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are awake and attentive, who practice meditation, who have insight, and who enjoy peaceful solitude are envied even by the gods.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with self-blame or despair, as it presents an ideal state that feels unattainable.

rows.text: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being born human is rare, living a mortal life is challenging, encountering genuine spiritual teachings is uncommon, and the appearance of an awakened being is extremely rare.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on life's difficulty may reinforce hopelessness in someone already experiencing despair, fresh grief, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The core instruction of all awakened beings is to avoid harmful actions, perform beneficial ones, and cleanse one's own mind.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on avoiding sin and purifying the mind may intensify feelings of guilt or inadequacy in those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair, and could feel invalidating to those processing anger or abuse.

rows.text: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Buddha teaches that enduring hardship without retaliation is the highest spiritual practice and the greatest peace. Someone who harms or insults others does not qualify as a true renunciant or holy person.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's praise of patience and long-suffering may invalidate the anger and need for self-protection of those experiencing abuse or relational harm, and its high standard could deepen shame or despair in those already struggling with self-blame or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts,—this is the teaching of the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Buddha's teaching is to avoid blaming or attacking others, to live with restraint and follow the law, to eat moderately, to spend time alone in quiet, and to keep the mind focused on lofty thoughts.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction 'not to blame' may invalidate the justified anger of abuse survivors or those experiencing relational harm, while the emphasis on solitude could deepen isolation for those in despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even unlimited wealth cannot satisfy desires; a wise person understands that desires bring only fleeting pleasure and lead to suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The statement that lusts cause pain and are unsatisfying may intensify shame or self-blame in those struggling with desire, and could feel dismissive to survivors of abuse where another's lust caused harm.

rows.text: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fully awakened person takes no pleasure even in the best worldly enjoyments; their joy comes only from eliminating all craving.
rows.riskNotes: The dismissal of even heavenly pleasures and the exaltation of desire's destruction could intensify despair or self-blame, and the phrase 'destruction of all desires' may be dangerously misread by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When people are afraid, they often look for safety in external things like mountains, forests, or sacred sites.

rows.text: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Turning to an unreliable source of security won't bring ultimate safety or release from all distress.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen hopelessness for someone in despair or grief by suggesting that even a sought refuge cannot deliver from pain.

rows.text: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths:—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who seeks safety in the Buddha, his teaching, and the community, and who clearly understands the four noble truths.
rows.riskNotes: The reference to taking refuge and seeing truths may feel exclusionary or like an unattainable standard for those in despair, self-blame, or religious trauma, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or alienation.

rows.text: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain;—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The four noble truths are stated: suffering, its cause in craving, its cessation, and the eightfold path that leads to the end of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The abstract framing of pain and its cessation may feel dismissive or overly simplistic to those in acute emotional distress, particularly those grieving, suicidal, or processing trauma.

rows.text: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A secure and supreme shelter exists; by taking refuge in it, a person is freed from all suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The promise of deliverance from all pain may feel dismissive or unrealistic to someone in acute distress, potentially deepening feelings of isolation or failure.

rows.text: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fully awakened person is extremely rare and not born just anywhere. When such a wise being does appear, the community where they are born flourishes.

rows.text: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is a joyful thing when an awakened person appears, when the true teaching is shared, when a community lives in harmony, and when peaceful people practice with dedication.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's focus on happiness can feel invalidating or highlight a painful gap for those in acute distress, especially if they feel unable to attain peace or devotion.

rows.text: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Honoring those who are truly worthy of respect—the awakened ones and their followers who have conquered inner evils and transcended suffering—brings a benefit so vast it cannot be calculated.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on homage and merit may intensify feelings of unworthiness in those experiencing shame or self-blame, and the mention of overcoming sorrow could deepen despair.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: We can live happily without hating those who hate us, remaining free from hatred even among hostile people.
rows.riskNotes: Urging freedom from hatred may pressure those who have been harmed to suppress their anger, potentially invalidating their pain.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person can remain happy and free from inner turmoil even while living among those who are troubled or suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This exhortation to be free from ailments while surrounded by suffering may feel dismissive or shaming to those in despair, grief, or self-blame, implying they should simply overcome their pain.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person can live happily by remaining free from greed, even while surrounded by greedy people.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's call to live happily and free from greed may feel dismissive or invalidating to those in acute distress, grief, or trauma, as it could be perceived as a platitude that ignores their pain.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: One can live happily without owning anything, like divine beings who thrive on joy alone.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's call to live happily despite having nothing may feel dismissive or invalidating to those in deep despair, fresh grief, or suicidal states, and could intensify shame for not feeling happy.

rows.text: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Winning creates resentment because the loser suffers. A person who lets go of both winning and losing finds contentment and is truly happy.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the pain of those who have experienced harm or loss, suggesting that contentment requires relinquishing any sense of injustice or grief, which can feel dismissive to victims of relational harm, those in grief, or individuals struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desire is a consuming fire, resentment is a losing bet, physical existence is inherently painful, and the highest contentment comes from inner stillness.
rows.riskNotes: The assertion that bodily pain is supreme and rest is the highest happiness may intensify hopelessness in those with chronic pain or suicidal thoughts, while framing hatred as a losing throw can feel invalidating to those processing justified anger from harm.

rows.text: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Craving is the most serious affliction, and clinging to the physical body brings the greatest suffering. Truly understanding this leads to the ultimate peace and happiness.
rows.riskNotes: The stark framing of the body as the greatest pain and hunger as the worst disease may deepen hopelessness for those in despair or with suicidal thoughts, and could feel dismissive of emotional anguish during fresh grief.

rows.text: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The best things in life are a healthy body, a satisfied mind, reliable relationships, and the peace of ultimate freedom.
rows.riskNotes: The idealization of health, contentment, and trust may feel invalidating to those struggling with illness, betrayal, or loss, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or grief.

rows.text: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has known the pleasantness of solitude and inner stillness no longer feels afraid or does wrong, as they delight in taking in the Dhamma.
rows.riskNotes: The implication that freedom from fear and sin is attained through solitude may lead individuals experiencing shame, panic, despair, or self-blame to feel inadequate or at fault for not having achieved such peace.

rows.text: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spending time with wise and virtuous people is good and brings lasting contentment. Staying away from foolish individuals is a source of true well-being.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's stark division of people into 'elect' and 'fools' can feel dismissive or blaming to those in pain, implying that unhappiness stems from one's company rather than validating complex emotional struggles.

rows.text: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spending time with foolish people brings lasting pain, like being with an enemy. Being with wise people is pleasant, like being with family.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel blaming to someone in an abusive or toxic environment, implying their suffering is due to their choice of company, which could exacerbate self-blame or shame.

rows.text: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is wise to seek the company of those who are intelligent, learned, patient, and dutiful, and to follow their example, as the moon follows the stars.
rows.riskNotes: For someone in shame or self-blame, this exhortation may feel like a reminder of their perceived inadequacy; for those harmed by trusted figures, it could invalidate their experience.

rows.text: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who chases empty distractions and avoids mental training, losing sight of what truly matters and clinging to pleasure, will eventually feel jealous of those who have dedicated themselves to meditation.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen feelings of shame and regret in someone already struggling with self-blame, despair, or grief, by framing their pursuit of comfort as a failure that will lead to future envy.

rows.text: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Actively pursuing pleasant experiences and avoiding unpleasant ones both lead to suffering. Not getting what you enjoy is painful, and encountering what you dislike is also painful.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen despair or self-blame in those already suffering, as it frames pain as an inevitable consequence of seeking pleasant experiences, potentially invalidating their distress.

rows.text: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Clinging to what you love brings pain when you lose it. A person who neither clings nor resents is free from inner bondage.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deeply wound someone grieving a loss by framing love as the cause of evil, and may invalidate the pain of those who have been harmed in relationships.
