rows.text: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Enjoying pleasant things brings sorrow and fear. A person who doesn't cling to pleasure avoids these.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may imply that suffering is self-inflicted through attachment to pleasure, which could feel blaming or invalidating to those in grief, shame, or despair.

rows.text: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Emotional attachment brings sorrow and anxiety. Without attachment, these painful feelings do not arise.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could hurt those grieving or in relational pain by implying their suffering stems from their own affection, potentially inducing self-blame or invalidation.

rows.text: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Strong desire leads to sadness and fear. Letting go of desire removes sadness and fear.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel blaming to those experiencing grief, fear, or shame, as it attributes these emotions to personal lust, potentially exacerbating self-blame and despair.

rows.text: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Attachment brings sorrow and fear; letting go of attachment frees a person from both.
rows.riskNotes: This passage can hurt those in grief, shame, or crisis by implying that love is the root of suffering and detachment is the only escape, which may feel blaming or dangerously dismissive of their pain.

rows.text: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Greed brings sorrow and fear; a person without greed experiences neither.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may imply that grief and fear are self-inflicted through greed, which could feel blaming or invalidating to someone in acute shame, grief, or despair.

rows.text: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is good, wise, fair, honest, and attends to their own responsibilities is valued by others.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with shame or despair, as it implies that only the virtuous are held dear.

rows.text: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has developed a genuine longing for liberation, whose mind is inwardly content, and who is no longer confused by clinging is said to be carried upward by the stream.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being free from love's bewilderment may invalidate those grieving a loss or healing from relational harm, and the emphasis on satisfaction could deepen feelings of inadequacy in despair or self-blame.

rows.text: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person returns safely after a long absence, their family and friends greet them with affection.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify feelings of loss for someone grieving a loved one who did not return safely, or deepen a sense of isolation in despair.

rows.text: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other;—as kinsmen receive a friend on his return.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone who has done good dies, their own good deeds welcome them in the next life, just as relatives welcome a friend coming home.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's mention of death and the afterlife could distress those in grief or with suicidal thoughts, and its focus on good works may intensify shame or self-blame in readers who feel they have not done enough.

rows.text: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should abandon anger and pride, and break free from all attachments. Suffering does not touch someone who is not clinging to identity or possessions.
rows.riskNotes: The message that suffering ends when one relinquishes anger and attachment can feel invalidating or like victim-blaming to those in pain, particularly if they are grieving, ashamed, or angry about harm done to them.

rows.text: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who can stop anger as it surges, like halting a runaway chariot, is truly in charge of themselves; everyone else only looks like they are holding the reins.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the anger of those who have been harmed, implying that true strength lies in suppression, which can be harmful for victims of abuse or those grieving.

rows.text: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Respond to hostility with kindness, to wrongdoing with goodness, to greed with generosity, and to dishonesty with truthfulness.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may pressure those experiencing anger from harm to suppress their feelings and forgive prematurely, potentially invalidating victims of abuse or those in grief.

rows.text: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being honest, not giving in to anger, and offering help when asked, even for small things, are three practices that bring a person close to a divine state.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to not yield to anger may invalidate the justified anger of someone who has been harmed, and the call to give when asked could be exploited in abusive dynamics.

rows.text: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who harm no one and maintain discipline over their actions attain a lasting state of peace where suffering ceases.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of non-injury and self-control may feel condemning to those in shame or self-blame, and the conditional promise of no suffering could deepen despair or suicidal feelings; it may also invalidate the pain of victims of relational harm or pressure those with rage to suppress their emotions.

rows.text: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who remain attentive, continuously learn, and pursue liberation will eventually overcome their compulsive desires.
rows.riskNotes: The call for ceaseless striving may overwhelm those in despair or self-blame, and the phrase 'passions will come to an end' could be dangerously misread by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People will find fault no matter how you speak—whether you stay quiet, talk a lot, or say just a little. No one in the world is free from criticism.
rows.riskNotes: The message that blame is inevitable can feel dismissive of personal pain and may deepen hopelessness or shame, especially for those already feeling judged or silenced.

rows.text: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No person has ever been, nor will ever be, universally blamed or universally praised. It is simply not possible.
rows.riskNotes: May feel dismissive to those who are persistently blamed or shamed, as it could imply their experience is not real or universal.

rows.text: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is genuinely wise and morally upright, recognized as such by discerning people, stands beyond reproach and is admired even by the highest beings.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of a flawless, praised person may deepen feelings of inadequacy and self-blame in someone already struggling with shame or despair.

rows.text: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Guard against anger that shows up in physical actions; restrain your body. Let go of harmful bodily deeds and instead do good through your body.
rows.riskNotes: The command to control anger and leave sins may intensify shame and self-blame, and could invalidate the justified anger of those harmed by others or recovering from abuse.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Angry speech is dangerous; one should restrain the tongue, abandon harmful words, and cultivate virtuous speech.
rows.riskNotes: This warning to control angry speech may intensify shame and self-blame in those already struggling with guilt, and could silence victims of abuse who need to speak out.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Guard against anger in your thoughts and discipline your mind. Let go of harmful mental habits and develop wholesome qualities with your mind.
rows.riskNotes: The command to control anger and abandon 'sins' may intensify shame and self-blame, and can invalidate the righteous anger of those harmed by others.

rows.text: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are truly wise keep their actions, words, and thoughts under careful control.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may be harmful to individuals in states of shame, self-blame, or crisis, as it can be perceived as a judgment on their lack of control, potentially intensifying feelings of inadequacy or invalidation.

rows.text: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is compared to a withered leaf, with death's messengers near, standing at the threshold of departure without any spiritual provisions for the journey ahead.
rows.riskNotes: This stark warning about death and unpreparedness could deepen despair and hopelessness in someone already feeling suicidal or grieving.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Rely on yourself, put in diligent effort, and cultivate understanding. When you have removed your inner defilements and are free from wrongdoing, you will attain a noble, elevated state.
rows.riskNotes: The call to be free from guilt and impurities may deepen shame and self-blame, while the demand to work hard could feel crushing to someone in despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Your life is ending, death is near, there is no place to rest along the way, and you have made no preparations for what comes next.
rows.riskNotes: This stark warning about death and unpreparedness could deepen despair, panic, or self-blame in someone already feeling hopeless or grieving.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Rely on your own efforts, be diligent, and develop understanding. When you have cleared away inner defilements and are free from wrongdoing, you will escape the cycle of rebirth and aging.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on impurities and guilt may intensify shame and self-blame, while the call to 'work hard' could overwhelm those in despair or panic, and the self-reliance metaphor might feel isolating for victims of relational harm.

rows.text: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person gradually removes their own flaws, just as a silversmith carefully and repeatedly removes impurities from silver.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of removing impurities may intensify feelings of inherent defectiveness in those experiencing shame or self-blame, and could be misinterpreted as self-annihilation by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as rust that forms on iron eventually corrodes and destroys the iron itself, a person's own harmful actions lead them to a painful and destructive path.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a harsh condemnation to someone already struggling with guilt or self-blame, reinforcing feelings of unworthiness and despair.

rows.text: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Neglect and laziness are flaws that ruin things: prayers become worthless when not repeated, houses fall apart without repair, the body deteriorates from sloth, and a guard fails due to thoughtlessness.
rows.riskNotes: The labeling of sloth as a taint could deepen feelings of worthlessness in those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A woman's misconduct and a benefactor's greed are both forms of impurity. All evil deeds are defiling, affecting both this life and the next.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's association of bad conduct specifically with women may shame or blame women, particularly those who have experienced abuse or are struggling with self-worth.

rows.text: But there is a taint worse than all taints,—ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The most serious flaw of all is not understanding the truth. Let go of that ignorance and become completely free of flaws.
rows.riskNotes: The call to throw off the taint of ignorance may be heard as a demand to fix one's inherent impurity, which could intensify shame and self-blame in those already feeling broken or contaminated.

rows.text: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has no sense of shame, who is bold and insulting, who causes trouble and acts wretchedly, finds life easy to get through.
rows.riskNotes: The harsh description of a shameless person may be internalized by those in shame or self-blame, and could feel invalidating to victims of relational harm who see their aggressors thriving.

rows.text: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is humble, always aims for what is pure, remains detached, calm, blameless, and discerning finds life challenging.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could discourage those in despair or self-blame by emphasizing the hardship of a virtuous life, potentially reinforcing feelings of hopelessness.

rows.text: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who harms living beings, deceives others, takes property without permission, or engages in sexual misconduct.
rows.riskNotes: This passage lists harmful actions without offering compassion or a path to change, which could intensify feelings of guilt, shame, or hopelessness in someone already struggling with self-blame or despair.

rows.text: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who habitually drinks alcohol destroys their own well-being and future, even in this life.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deeply wound someone struggling with alcohol addiction or shame, as it frames their behavior as self-destructive and may reinforce feelings of worthlessness.

rows.text: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks self-control is in a bad situation; greed and immoral behavior can lead to prolonged suffering.
rows.riskNotes: This warning may feel like blame or a threat to those already struggling with shame, despair, or grief, potentially deepening feelings of hopelessness or self-condemnation.

rows.text: The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People give to others based on their own faith or preference. A person who frets over what others receive will have no peace, day or night.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could be harmful to those struggling with shame or self-blame, as it may feel like a dismissal of their pain, and the promise of no rest could deepen despair or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who fully uproots a persistent feeling experiences lasting peace, undisturbed by time of day.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to destroy a feeling at its root can feel like a demand to suppress or eliminate pain, which may be invalidating or overwhelming for someone in acute distress or grief.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Intense desire is as destructive as a fire, hatred as dangerous as a shark, foolishness as entangling as a trap, and greed as overpowering as a flood.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid metaphors of destructive forces can intensify feelings of shame, self-blame, or despair in those already struggling with these emotions, and may invalidate righteous anger or grief.

rows.text: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People easily notice the faults of others, but struggle to see their own; they hide their own mistakes like a dishonest gambler concealing a bad die.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may intensify self-condemnation in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it highlights the difficulty of seeing one's own faults.

rows.text: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person constantly finds fault with others and takes offense easily, their own negative emotions increase, and they remain far from freeing themselves from those emotions.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like a condemnation to those already burdened by self-blame or despair, and could invalidate the justified anger of those who have been harmed.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no magical shortcut to spiritual growth, and performing external rituals doesn't make someone a true seeker. Most people are fascinated by superficial appearances, but the awakened are free from that kind of vanity.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could be interpreted as a judgment on one's spiritual worth, potentially deepening feelings of shame, self-blame, or despair in those already struggling with self-criticism.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not a true renunciant merely by outward behavior. All beings are subject to change, but those who are fully awake remain steady and undisturbed.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt statement of impermanence may deepen despair or grief, and the emphasis on inner state over outward acts could fuel self-blame in those already feeling inadequate.

rows.text: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True justice isn't about forcing outcomes; it comes from understanding right and wrong, guiding others with fairness and law, not violence, and being wise and law-abiding.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those who have experienced abuse or injustice, as it could be interpreted as discouraging righteous anger or self-defense.

rows.text: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person isn't truly wise just because they talk a lot. Real wisdom is shown by patience and freedom from hatred and fear.
rows.riskNotes: The call to be free from hatred may feel invalidating to someone who is rightfully angry after being harmed, especially if they are disclosing abuse or processing relational trauma.

rows.text: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not a genuine follower of the teachings simply by speaking a lot about them. Even someone who has studied little but directly understands and lives the teachings, never neglecting them, is a true follower.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those already struggling with shame or self-blame, as it contrasts mere talk with true embodiment of the law.

rows.text: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Having grey hair doesn't make someone a wise elder; a person can be old in years but still be called 'old in vain' if they lack true understanding.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'old in vain' may deepen feelings of worthlessness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair about their life's meaning.

rows.text: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives with honesty, moral integrity, kindness, self-control, balance, inner purity, and insight is what truly makes someone respected as an elder.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being free from impurity and possessing virtue may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those already burdened by shame, self-blame, or despair, potentially reinforcing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Respectability doesn't come from talking a lot or having a nice appearance when a person is envious, greedy, and untrustworthy.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could reinforce feelings of worthlessness in someone struggling with shame or self-blame, especially if they identify with the negative traits mentioned.

rows.text: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completely uprooted defilements, especially hatred, and has become wise is considered truly respectable.
rows.riskNotes: The passage may imply that those who still experience hatred are not respectable, which could deepen shame or self-blame in someone struggling with anger or trauma.

rows.text: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who shaves their head but remains undisciplined, tells lies, and is enslaved by desire and greed is not a genuine ascetic.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's judgment of inner failings may intensify feelings of shame and self-blame in those already struggling with self-worth.

rows.text: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently calms and overcomes harmful tendencies, no matter how minor or major, is truly peaceful because they have eliminated all such negativity.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of 'quieting evil' may be internalized as self-condemnation or pressure to suppress valid emotions, risking harm for those in shame, self-blame, or despair, and could feel dismissive to survivors of abuse or relational harm.

rows.text: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Simply relying on others for material support does not make someone a true spiritual practitioner. A genuine practitioner is one who fully embraces and lives by the entire teaching, not just the outward form of begging.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deepen feelings of inadequacy for someone already struggling with self-blame or shame about their spiritual practice, as it emphasizes inner transformation over outward actions.

rows.text: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has transcended conventional notions of good and evil, lives with purity and self-control, and navigates life with insight is considered a true monk.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of transcending good and evil and being chaste may feel invalidating or shaming to those in crisis, especially survivors of abuse or those grappling with self-blame.

rows.text: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person does not become a sage just by staying silent if they lack understanding. A true sage is someone who carefully weighs options and deliberately chooses what is good while avoiding what is harmful.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on choosing good and avoiding evil may feel like a moral judgment to those in shame or self-blame, and the dismissal of 'foolish and ignorant' silence could invalidate someone's quiet suffering.

rows.text: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is not considered noble because they harm living beings; rather, one is called noble because they show compassion to all living beings.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may wound those in shame or self-blame by implying they are not noble if they have caused harm, and may invalidate the anger of those harmed by urging pity toward all.

rows.text: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External religious practices like discipline, learning, meditation, or solitude do not by themselves bring the ultimate peace of liberation. One should not feel assured until all craving has been completely eliminated.
rows.riskNotes: The warning against premature confidence and the demand to extinguish desires may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those already burdened by shame or self-blame, and could invalidate the natural grief or anger of someone in pain.

rows.text: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The eightfold path is the finest way to live, the four noble truths are the finest teachings, freedom from craving is the finest quality, and a person with clear insight is the finest kind of person.
rows.riskNotes: The exaltation of passionlessness may invalidate the emotional experiences of those in grief, shame, or anger, potentially making them feel their natural responses are unworthy.

rows.text: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is only one path that leads to the purification of understanding. Follow that path; all other paths are deceptive distractions.
rows.riskNotes: The absolute claim of a single path and dismissal of alternatives as deceit may feel condemning or invalidating to those in shame, despair, grief, or crisis, potentially deepening feelings of failure or hopelessness.

rows.text: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Following this path brings an end to suffering. The speaker teaches it after having understood how to remove the sources of pain.
rows.riskNotes: The promise of ending pain through a specific path may feel invalidating or like a simplistic solution to someone in deep despair, grief, or self-blame, potentially increasing feelings of failure.

rows.text: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Liberation depends on your own effort; teachers can only show the path. Those who are mindful and follow it free themselves from inner bondage.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on personal effort may feel like blame or added pressure to someone already struggling with shame, despair, or trauma, implying their suffering is due to insufficient effort.

rows.text: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who truly sees that all things are impermanent becomes undisturbed by suffering; this is the path to inner purity.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the pain of those grieving, in despair, or experiencing suicidal thoughts by suggesting passivity as a solution, and could be harmful for abuse survivors by discouraging action.

rows.text: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Everything that arises is painful. When someone truly sees this, they stop resisting pain, and that leads to inner purity.
rows.riskNotes: The claim that all created things are grief and pain and that passivity in pain leads to purity can feel invalidating and hopeless, potentially worsening despair, grief, or suicidal ideation, and discouraging those in harmful situations from seeking change.

rows.text: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person truly understands that all things are impermanent and without solid reality, they stop struggling against pain and remain undisturbed. This insight is the path to inner freedom.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may invalidate the suffering of those in acute distress by suggesting their pain is unreal and they should passively accept it, potentially worsening feelings of shame, grief, or despair.

rows.text: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is lazy, doesn't get going when needed, and has weak determination and thinking will never attain true understanding.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could shame someone who is struggling with motivation due to depression, grief, or trauma, making them feel like a failure for not being productive.

rows.text: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should guard their words, keep their mind disciplined, and avoid harmful physical actions. By keeping these three areas pure, they follow the path taught by the wise.
rows.riskNotes: The call to perfect self-restraint may deepen shame and self-blame in those already burdened by guilt or trauma, and can feel dismissive or victim-blaming to someone who has experienced relational harm.

rows.text: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Knowledge grows through consistent effort and fades when effort is absent. Recognizing this, one should act in a way that allows understanding to develop.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on personal zeal may feel like blame or an impossible demand to someone already struggling with self-blame, despair, or acute distress.

rows.text: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: You must completely uproot desire, not just suppress parts of it. Desire is dangerous, and only by eliminating it entirely can you be free.
rows.riskNotes: The warning about danger from lust and the imperative to cut it down may intensify shame and self-blame in those already struggling with guilt, and the violent imagery could distress individuals in despair or with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: As long as a person has even a trace of romantic or sexual desire for others, their mind remains trapped, like a calf tied to its mother by the need for milk.
rows.riskNotes: Framing love as bondage can intensify shame and self-blame in those with relational trauma, and may feel invalidating or cruel to someone grieving a partner or disclosing abuse.

rows.text: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Remove self-centered attachment as you would pluck an autumn lotus. Value the path to inner peace; the Buddha has shown the way to liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The command to 'cut out the love of self' could be misinterpreted as self-rejection or self-harm, potentially deepening shame, self-blame, or despair in vulnerable individuals.

rows.text: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool makes detailed plans for the coming seasons but never considers that death might intervene.
rows.riskNotes: Calling someone a fool for ignoring death can intensify shame and self-blame, while the blunt mention of mortality may trigger panic, deepen despair, or distress those grieving or experiencing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person distracted by praise and worldly possessions can be suddenly overtaken by death, like a sleeping village swept away by a flood.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid imagery of death as a sudden flood could deeply distress those grieving a recent loss, experiencing suicidal thoughts, or feeling overwhelmed by despair or panic.

rows.text: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When death comes, family and relatives are unable to offer any real protection or assistance.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen despair or hopelessness in someone grieving a recent loss or struggling with suicidal thoughts, as it emphasizes the utter isolation of death.

rows.text: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who understands the path should act without delay to remove whatever blocks their progress toward liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The call to quickly clear the way may feel like an impossible demand to those in despair or self-blame, reinforcing feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If letting go of a smaller enjoyment brings a much larger one, a wise person will give up the smaller and pursue the larger.
rows.riskNotes: The suggestion to trade a small pleasure for a greater one may feel dismissive of present pain, implying that current suffering is a trivial indulgence to be overcome, which can deepen feelings of shame, hopelessness, or grief.

rows.text: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who tries to gain happiness by harming others becomes trapped in hatred and cannot free themselves from it.
rows.riskNotes: This warning can feel like a condemnation to those already blaming themselves or grappling with anger after harm, potentially deepening shame or despair.

rows.text: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are careless and thoughtless neglect their duties and do what they shouldn't, and their desires only grow stronger.
rows.riskNotes: The critical tone may be internalized as blame by those already feeling shame or despair, potentially worsening self-judgment.

rows.text: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who constantly pay attention to their physical actions, avoid harmful behaviors, and consistently do what is beneficial eventually eliminate all their cravings.
rows.riskNotes: The moralistic 'ought' language can deepen self-blame and shame, while the promise of desires ending may be dangerously misread by someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly liberated person remains untouched by wrongdoing even after metaphorically destroying the deepest roots of suffering—craving, conceit, and attachment to sensory experience.
rows.riskNotes: The graphic mention of killing family and destroying a kingdom can be deeply triggering for those in grief, shame, or trauma, and may be misread as excusing violence.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completely uprooted the deepest inner causes of suffering—like compulsive desire and the illusion of a fixed self—remains untouched by harm, no matter how violent that inner work appears.
rows.riskNotes: The literal mention of killing parents and being 'scatheless' can be deeply distressing for someone in fresh grief, suicidal ideation, or self-blame, and may feel like a dismissal of real harm for victims of relational violence.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Buddha's students remain continuously alert, and their thinking is always centered on the Buddha.
rows.riskNotes: The idealized description of constant mindfulness may intensify feelings of inadequacy or failure in someone already struggling with shame, despair, or grief.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who follow the Buddha stay attentive, with their minds continually directed toward the truth he taught.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may make someone struggling with self-blame or despair feel inadequate by contrasting their state with the ideal of constant wakefulness.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The followers of the Buddha remain alert and keep their minds focused on the community of practitioners at all times.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of constant wakefulness and single-minded devotion may intensify feelings of inadequacy or failure in someone already struggling with self-blame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The followers of Gotama stay alert, keeping their attention continuously on the body.
rows.riskNotes: The call to constant bodily awareness may feel like an unattainable demand for those in despair or self-blame, and focusing on the body could be triggering for individuals with trauma or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Those who follow the Buddha stay attentive, and their inner life continually finds happiness in caring for others.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of constant wakefulness and delight in compassion may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those struggling with shame, grief, or self-blame, and may pressure abuse survivors to suppress anger.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Buddha's students stay attentive, and their minds find constant pleasure in meditative practice.
rows.riskNotes: This idealized depiction of constant mindfulness may intensify feelings of failure or inadequacy in those struggling with despair or self-blame.

rows.text: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Renouncing the world is hard, and so is indulging in it. Monastic life is difficult, household life is painful, and sharing possessions brings distress. The wandering mendicant is afflicted with pain, so the advice is to avoid that path to escape suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's bleak portrayal of life's inherent pain and its counsel to avoid a spiritual path could intensify hopelessness in those already despairing or grieving, and may feel dismissive to victims of relational harm.

rows.text: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is trustworthy, morally upright, well-known, and prosperous is honored in any community they settle in.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's celebration of a respected, wealthy, virtuous man may deepen feelings of inadequacy and shame in those who feel they lack these qualities, especially if they are already in a state of self-blame or despair.

rows.text: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Virtuous individuals stand out clearly, like snow-capped mountains seen from afar, while those who are unvirtuous remain hidden and ineffective, like arrows shot in the darkness.
rows.riskNotes: The stark good/bad dichotomy may deepen shame and self-blame in those already feeling worthless or unseen, and could feel invalidating to those who have been harmed by others.

rows.text: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who steadily trains in solitude and self-restraint, mastering themselves, will experience happiness when all desires are ended, like living alone in a forest.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on solitary practice and subduing oneself may intensify feelings of isolation, self-blame, or hopelessness in those already in crisis, and could invalidate the pain of grief, abuse, or righteous anger.

rows.text: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who tells lies or denies their own actions will face severe consequences after death, as both are considered evil.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deeply wound someone in shame or self-blame by threatening hell for past lies, reinforcing feelings of being irredeemably evil.

rows.text: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Some who wear the monk's robe lack discipline and act harmfully; their own bad deeds bring them to suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of hell for evil deeds may intensify self-condemnation in those already feeling shame, despair, or suicidal, and could be retraumatizing for abuse survivors who blame themselves.

rows.text: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is better to endure extreme physical pain than to be an immoral and undisciplined person who takes support from others.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's harsh condemnation of those who receive charity could deeply wound individuals struggling with feelings of worthlessness, self-blame, or suicidal ideation, as it frames them as undeserving of support.

rows.text: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife,—a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A careless person who desires another's spouse will end up with a ruined reputation, sleepless nights, punishment, and a terrible afterlife.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of punishment and hell could deepen feelings of shame, panic, and hopelessness in someone already burdened by guilt or self-blame.

rows.text: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Entertaining thoughts of a neighbor's spouse brings a bad reputation, a harmful path, brief pleasure tainted by fear, and harsh penalties from rulers; thus one should not indulge such thoughts.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of hell and punishment for adultery can deepen shame and self-blame in those with sexual regrets, and may feel dismissive or triggering for victims of infidelity.

rows.text: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a blade of grass can cut the hand when grasped improperly, ascetic practices done incorrectly lead to harmful outcomes.
rows.riskNotes: The warning of hell for improper practice may intensify self-blame and despair in those already feeling inadequate or suffering.

rows.text: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Careless actions, broken promises, and half-hearted commitment to practice don't produce worthwhile results.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could reinforce feelings of failure and unworthiness in someone experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When something needs to be done, do it with full commitment and energy. A person who is careless only stirs up their own desires and makes their situation worse.
rows.riskNotes: The call to vigorous action may overwhelm those in crisis, intensifying feelings of failure, pressure, or hopelessness.

rows.text: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wrong actions cause remorse afterward, so they are best avoided. Right actions do not cause remorse, so they are worth doing.
rows.riskNotes: May intensify guilt and regret in those already burdened by shame or self-blame over past actions.

rows.text: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should guard themselves as carefully as a well-defended fortress, with inner and outer protections. Every moment matters; those who let the right moment slip will experience pain.
rows.riskNotes: The threat of hell and suffering for missed moments can deepen self-blame and despair, while the guarding metaphor may feel like victim-blaming to those who have experienced relational harm.

rows.text: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Some people feel shame about things that aren't shameful, and feel no shame about things that are genuinely wrong. Holding mistaken beliefs, they end up on a harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's condemnatory framing of misplaced shame and the threat of an 'evil path' could deepen self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling shame or despair.

rows.text: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are afraid of things that aren't dangerous and aren't afraid of things that are truly harmful end up holding wrong beliefs and heading down a destructive path.
rows.riskNotes: This warning about misdirected fear and an evil path could intensify self-blame and despair in someone already feeling shame or hopelessness, and may invalidate the natural fear of a grieving person.

rows.text: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who prohibit things that don't need prohibiting and fail to prohibit things that do, holding wrong views, end up on a harmful path.
rows.riskNotes: The warning of an 'evil path' may intensify feelings of guilt and hopelessness in those already struggling with self-blame or despair.

rows.text: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.
