rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who clearly understand what is morally wrong and what is not, and who follow the true teaching, find the right way to live.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on knowing forbidden actions may intensify self-condemnation in those already burdened by shame or self-blame, and could feel invalidating to victims of relational harm who were silenced by rigid moral rules.

rows.text: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: I will endure harsh treatment quietly, like a war elephant withstands arrows, because people often behave badly.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may encourage victims of abuse to silently endure harm, which could be damaging for those in abusive situations or processing trauma.

rows.text: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who can endure harsh words without reacting is like a well-trained elephant that can be guided into battle; such self-control is the highest quality.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could harm those who have experienced abuse or relational harm by implying they should silently endure mistreatment.

rows.text: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Tamed animals like mules, horses, and elephants are valuable, but a person who has trained their own mind and behavior is far more valuable.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-taming may feel like an added burden or implicit criticism to those already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No one reaches liberation by depending on untamed animals; only a person who has tamed themselves can arrive there, using their own disciplined self as the vehicle.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-taming may be heard as blame by those in self-blame or shame, and could invalidate the pain of those who have been harmed by others.

rows.text: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A powerful elephant, hard to control, refuses food when tied up and longs for the forest where it used to roam.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of a bound creature refusing food and longing for a lost home may intensify feelings of entrapment and hopelessness in those experiencing despair, suicidal ideation, or grief.

rows.text: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who overeats, sleeps too much, and lounges around is acting foolishly. Like a pig fed on garbage, they will be reborn again and again.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's harsh condemnation of bodily appetites and laziness could deeply wound someone struggling with shame, self-blame, or suicidal thoughts, reinforcing feelings of worthlessness.

rows.text: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker acknowledges that their mind once roamed freely, but now they resolve to discipline it firmly, just as a mahout controls a wild elephant with a hook.
rows.riskNotes: The forceful imagery of taming a furious elephant with a hook may feel like a demand for harsh self-control, potentially exacerbating feelings of shame, self-blame, or despair in those already struggling with inner turmoil or trauma.

rows.text: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Don't be careless; pay attention to your own mind. Pull yourself out of harmful patterns, just as an elephant stuck in mud struggles to free itself.
rows.riskNotes: The command to extract oneself from an 'evil way' can deepen shame and self-blame in those already feeling broken, and may feel invalidating to victims of harm or those in grief.

rows.text: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If a person finds a sensible, wise, and disciplined companion, they can journey together, overcoming dangers, with happiness and mutual consideration.

rows.text: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind,—like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: If you can't find a wise and steady companion, it's better to be on your own, like someone who has walked away from power or an elephant living independently in nature.
rows.riskNotes: The advice to walk alone may be misinterpreted as endorsing isolation, which could deepen despair or suicidal ideation in those already feeling disconnected.

rows.text: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living alone is better than keeping company with a foolish person. One should walk a solitary path, avoid wrongdoing, and be content with little, like an elephant roaming freely in the forest.
rows.riskNotes: The advice to shun companionship and walk alone may intensify feelings of isolation and self-blame in those already struggling with shame, grief, or suicidal thoughts, and could dismiss the need for connection after relational trauma.

rows.text: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Having friends around when you need them feels good. Enjoying things, whatever the reason, is pleasant. Doing good brings comfort when you face death. And releasing all grief is the most pleasant thing of all.
rows.riskNotes: The suggestion that giving up grief is pleasant may feel dismissive to those in fresh grief or despair, and the mention of death could unsettle someone with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being a mother or father is a pleasant experience in life, and so is being a monk or a holy person.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may distress those with painful family relationships, grief over a parent, or feelings of inadequacy, as it idealizes parental roles without acknowledging suffering.

rows.text: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living with integrity that lasts into old age feels good. So does having a steady trust, gaining insight, and steering clear of wrongdoing.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of 'sins' may intensify feelings of guilt and unworthiness in those experiencing acute shame, self-blame, or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person lacks mindfulness, their desires keep growing and entangle them, driving them to chase pleasures life after life, like a monkey leaping from tree to tree in search of fruit.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of thoughtless craving and endless rebirth may feel like a condemnation to those in shame or self-blame, and the image of running from life to life could exacerbate despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When intense, poisonous craving takes hold of a person, their suffering grows rapidly, like the fast-spreading Birana grass.
rows.riskNotes: The warning that craving increases suffering may feel like blame to those in shame or self-blame, and the imagery of poison and growing suffering could deepen despair or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person overcomes the powerful force of craving, which is hard to defeat, all suffering slides away from them, just as water beads roll off a lotus leaf.
rows.riskNotes: It can sound like suffering is one's own fault for not overcoming craving, which may feel invalidating or blaming to those in shame, grief, or trauma.

rows.text: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: To stop being repeatedly overwhelmed by temptation, one must eliminate craving at its source, similar to digging up a plant to extract its root.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being crushed repeatedly if one fails to uproot thirst can feel like a threat, potentially worsening self-blame, despair, or panic in someone already fragile.

rows.text: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a tree grows back if its root is unharmed, suffering will keep arising again and again unless the deep-seated craving that fuels it is completely removed.
rows.riskNotes: The message that pain will inevitably return unless inner thirst is destroyed can feel hopeless and blaming, potentially deepening despair, self-blame, or shame in vulnerable readers.

rows.text: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person's craving for pleasure is intense and flows through many channels, those desires sweep them away like a flood, carrying them toward suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's labeling of desire-driven people as 'misguided' and doomed to be carried away could intensify feelings of shame, self-blame, and hopelessness in those already struggling with these emotions.

rows.text: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Craving spreads through the mind like a creeping vine. When you notice it starting to grow, cut it at the root with clear understanding.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of cutting the root of passion may feel like an indictment of natural desires, potentially exacerbating shame or self-blame, and the imagery of cutting could be distressing for those with suicidal ideation.

rows.text: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People chase extravagant pleasures and get lost in desire; this leads them to be born and die over and over again.
rows.riskNotes: The warning about lust and repeated suffering may intensify shame and self-blame in those already struggling with desire, and the mention of decay could distress someone grieving or suicidal.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When craving takes over, people behave like a rabbit caught in a snare—they become entangled in attachments and experience repeated suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being trapped and endlessly suffering may intensify hopelessness in those with despair or suicidal ideation, and the implication that craving causes pain could feel blaming to someone in self-blame or shame.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People driven by craving run around like a rabbit caught in a snare. A monk should therefore remove craving by striving for a state free of desire.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of being a snared hare driven by thirst could intensify feelings of entrapment and self-blame in those already struggling with shame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has freed themselves from craving but then willingly returns to it, even after liberation, only ends up in bondage again.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could feel like a harsh judgment to someone struggling with relapse or addiction, potentially deepening shame and despair.

rows.text: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Chains made of iron, wood, or hemp are not the strongest kind of bond. The anxious care for jewels, rings, children, and a spouse binds a person much more tightly.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may deeply hurt those grieving a loved one or feeling despair, as it frames cherished relationships as fetters, potentially invalidating their love and pain.

rows.text: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A powerful bond, flexible yet hard to break, keeps people tied to desires and pleasures. Those who finally sever it become free from worry and leave worldly attachments behind.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of cutting a fetter and leaving the world could be misinterpreted as advocating self-harm or emotional numbing, which may deeply wound someone in despair, grief, or suicidal crisis.

rows.text: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People driven by their own desires are like a spider moving along a web it spun itself. When they cut through that web, the wise let go of all clinging and live without distress.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of cutting off all affection and leaving the world may be distressing for those grieving a loss or experiencing suicidal thoughts, and the label 'slaves to passions' could intensify shame in someone already struggling with self-blame.

rows.text: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Letting go of attachment to past, future, and present leads to liberation. A mind that is entirely free does not return to the cycle of birth and death.
rows.riskNotes: The directive to give up all attachments may feel invalidating or dismissive to those in acute distress, especially if they interpret it as a call to abandon their pain or trauma.

rows.text: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person is plagued by uncertainty, driven by intense desires, and always chasing pleasure, their cravings only intensify and they bind themselves more tightly.
rows.riskNotes: This warning about craving leading to stronger fetters may feel like blame or condemnation to someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or grief over lost attachments.

rows.text: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, &c.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who enjoys clearing up confusion and keeps their attention on the unpleasant sides of life, like the body's unattractive nature, will break free from the grip of craving and ignorance.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to dwell on the impurity of the body could intensify self-loathing or body shame in someone already struggling with self-blame or despair.

rows.text: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has reached the highest spiritual attainment is free from fear and craving, has ended all suffering, and will not be reborn.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of a perfected being may deepen feelings of inadequacy and self-blame in those already struggling with shame or despair.

rows.text: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person free from craving and attachment, who fully understands the teachings and their deeper meaning, has reached the final state and is called a great sage.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of detachment from affection and thirst may feel invalidating to those grieving, in despair, or processing relational harm, and could exacerbate self-blame in those already feeling inadequate.

rows.text: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has overcome all, knows all, and is free from impurity in every condition of life; who has abandoned everything and destroyed craving, is liberated. Having realized this for themselves, they wonder who there is to teach.
rows.riskNotes: This triumphant declaration of personal mastery may deepen feelings of inadequacy, shame, or hopelessness in those struggling with self-blame, grief, or trauma, and could be misinterpreted by someone in suicidal crisis.

rows.text: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The Dhamma is the greatest gift, the sweetest experience, and the highest joy; ending craving eliminates all suffering.
rows.riskNotes: To someone in despair or fresh grief, this passage may feel dismissive of their pain by implying that simply extinguishing craving will overcome all suffering.

rows.text: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who chases after pleasures without seeking deeper meaning ends up harming themselves, as if they were their own worst enemy.
rows.riskNotes: This passage could deepen self-blame and hopelessness in someone already feeling like their own enemy, especially those in despair or with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin a field, desire ruins a person. Giving to someone who has overcome desire brings great reward.
rows.riskNotes: The claim that passion damages mankind can feel like an indictment of one's own emotions, potentially deepening shame, self-blame, or hopelessness in those already struggling with grief, trauma, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Hatred damages a person the way weeds damage a crop. Giving to people who are free from hatred is especially fruitful.
rows.riskNotes: The statement that hatred damages mankind may feel invalidating or blaming to those experiencing justified anger or self-hatred, potentially worsening shame or rage.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin a field, vanity ruins a person. Giving to those who are free from vanity brings great reward.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of being 'damaged by vanity' may feel like a personal condemnation to those struggling with shame or self-worth, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Weeds ruin crops, and desire ruins people. Giving to those who have overcome desire brings great benefit.
rows.riskNotes: The statement that lust damages mankind may deepen shame or self-blame in those grappling with desire or sexual trauma, and the label 'damaged' could be harmful to someone in crisis.

rows.text: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Exercising control over the senses—sight, hearing, smell, and speech—is beneficial.
rows.riskNotes: The call for restraint can feel like an injunction to suppress natural reactions, potentially intensifying shame, self-blame, or emotional overwhelm in vulnerable individuals.

rows.text: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Exercising control over physical actions, speech, and thoughts is beneficial. A person who practices such restraint in all aspects of life becomes free from suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on restraint as the path to freedom from pain could feel invalidating or blaming to those who are suffering acutely, especially if they interpret their pain as a personal failure of discipline.

rows.text: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who keeps their actions and words in check, who finds inner joy and calm, and who is content with a simple, solitary life is considered a genuine practitioner.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-control and solitude may feel like a demand for perfection, potentially deepening shame or despair in those struggling with self-blame or relational harm.

rows.text: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who guards his speech, speaks with insight and calm, and teaches the meaning and the doctrine, his words are pleasing.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on controlled, sweet speech may feel like a demand to suppress authentic distress, potentially invalidating those in pain from shame, self-blame, or relational trauma.

rows.text: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives by the teachings, enjoys them, reflects on them, and acts accordingly will stay on the right path and never lose their way.
rows.riskNotes: The promise of never falling away may feel like an unattainable standard to someone struggling with shame or self-blame, potentially deepening feelings of failure.

rows.text: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Discontent with one's own situation and envy toward others disrupt inner peace.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel dismissive or shaming to someone in acute distress, as it could be heard as a command to suppress legitimate pain or dissatisfaction, potentially deepening feelings of inadequacy or isolation.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who does not look down on the little he receives, lives a pure life, and is not lazy is praised even by the gods.
rows.riskNotes: The praise of contentment with little may be felt as a moral demand, potentially shaming those who struggle with self-blame, despair, or feel trapped in abusive circumstances.

rows.text: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who does not cling to a fixed sense of self or physical identity, and who does not mourn what has passed, is what it means to be a true renunciant.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction not to grieve may feel invalidating to those in fresh grief, despair, or suicidal ideation, and could dismiss the pain of relational harm.

rows.text: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who is kind and lives calmly according to the Buddha's teaching attains Nirvana, the end of craving and lasting happiness.
rows.riskNotes: The promise of peace through calm kindness and cessation of desires may feel like an impossible standard that blames those in shame or despair, invalidates grief and righteous anger, and could be dangerously misread as endorsing self-harm in suicidal states.

rows.text: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a boat moves swiftly when emptied of water, a person reaches liberation by removing craving and hatred.
rows.riskNotes: The command to cut off passion and hatred may feel dismissive of legitimate grief or anger, and the urgency to 'go quickly' could be triggering for someone in despair or with suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A practitioner must detach from the five senses, leave them behind, and go beyond them. When someone escapes the five chains that bind them, they are said to have crossed over the torrent of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The directive to 'cut off' senses may be triggering for those with self-harm or suicidal ideation, and the call to detach can feel dismissive or invalidating to individuals in grief, shame, or processing trauma.

rows.text: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant is told to meditate and not be careless, and to avoid focusing on pleasure, so that they do not experience intense suffering as a result of their heedlessness.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid threat of hellish punishment could intensify feelings of shame, despair, or self-blame, and may be particularly harmful for those with religious trauma or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Understanding without practice remains shallow, and meditation without insight is empty. A person who develops both is close to liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on knowledge and meditation as prerequisites for Nirvana could make someone in self-blame or despair feel they are failing, reinforcing a sense of inadequacy and distance from peace.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who has gone to a quiet place and calmed his mind experiences an extraordinary joy when he clearly understands the truth.
rows.riskNotes: The description of transcendent delight in solitude may deepen feelings of isolation and hopelessness for those in despair or grieving.

rows.text: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person observes the arising and ceasing of the physical and mental aggregates, they experience the happiness and joy of those who have realized the unconditioned.
rows.riskNotes: The focus on the body's destruction may intensify hopelessness in those grieving or contemplating suicide, and the promise of joy may feel unreachable for someone in despair.

rows.text: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person who has left home life should start by guarding their senses, being content with little, following moral guidelines, and keeping company with good friends who live purely and are not lazy.
rows.riskNotes: The exhortation to be content and restrained may feel like an impossible demand to someone in deep suffering, intensifying feelings of inadequacy or invalidating their pain.

rows.text: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who practices generosity and carries out their duties fully will experience profound happiness and overcome suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The demand for perfection in duties may deepen self-blame or shame, and the phrase 'make an end of suffering' could be dangerously misread by someone considering suicide.

rows.text: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as the Vassika plant drops its withered flowers, a person should let go of strong desire and hostility.
rows.riskNotes: The command to shed passion and hatred can feel like a dismissal of valid emotions, potentially increasing shame or pressure in those already struggling.

rows.text: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who has calmed his actions, speech, and thoughts, who is composed and has let go of worldly temptations, is truly at peace.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of quietude and rejection of worldly baits may feel invalidating to those in acute distress, grief, or trauma, as it could be perceived as urging suppression of natural emotions.

rows.text: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person needs to stir themselves into action and look honestly at their own mind. When they guard themselves and stay alert, they can live with ease.
rows.riskNotes: The call to self-examination may intensify self-blame and shame in those already struggling with guilt or despair.

rows.text: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The only authority over you is you, and the only safe place is within yourself. So learn to control your own behavior the way a skilled trader controls a spirited horse.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self as sole refuge and the command to curb oneself may deepen feelings of isolation and self-blame in those experiencing shame, despair, or suicidal ideation, and could feel invalidating to abuse survivors or those needing external support.

rows.text: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A monk who is joyful and calm in the Buddha's teaching reaches Nirvana, where all wanting stops and genuine happiness is found.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating to those in acute distress, as it presents a state of delight and calm that can seem unattainable or blameful when suffering.

rows.text: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A young person who devotes themselves to the Buddha's way of life brings light and clarity to the world, like the moon when no clouds hide it.

rows.text: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Bravely halt the flow of craving and push away desires. When you fully grasp the breaking apart of everything conditioned, you will know the unconditioned.
rows.riskNotes: The command to valiantly stop desires and the mention of destruction may feel pressuring and invalidating to those in grief, despair, or self-blame, and could discourage natural anger in victims of harm.

rows.text: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone has fully developed both ethical discipline and meditative concentration, all mental fetters fall away for the one who has gained insight.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's promise of vanishing bonds may feel dismissive of current pain, potentially exacerbating feelings of shame, despair, or grief in those who are suffering acutely.

rows.text: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who doesn't cling to any state or idea, whether worldly or transcendent, lives without fear and is completely free.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being 'fearless and unshackled' may feel invalidating or like an impossible standard to those in acute emotional pain, grief, or shame.

rows.text: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is mindful, blameless, calm, diligent, free from craving, and has reached the ultimate spiritual goal is truly an enlightened being.
rows.riskNotes: The description of an ideal blameless and passionless state may intensify feelings of failure and unworthiness in those experiencing shame, grief, or trauma, and could feel dismissive of legitimate emotional pain.

rows.text: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: While the sun, moon, a warrior in armor, and a meditating sage each shine in specific circumstances, a fully awakened person radiates continuously, day and night.

rows.text: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is called a Brahmana because they have removed evil, a Samana because they live calmly, and a pilgrim because they have let go of inner impurities.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on being rid of evil and impurities may intensify feelings of shame, self-blame, or despair in those already struggling with a sense of inherent unworthiness.

rows.text: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: It is wrong to attack a person of spiritual attainment, but if such a person is attacked, it is even worse for them to retaliate.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may harm victims of aggression by implying that retaliation brings greater suffering, potentially silencing those who need to defend themselves or seek justice, and can deepen self-blame or shame in those already struggling.

rows.text: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Restraining the mind from chasing pleasures brings significant benefit. When the desire to harm others completely disappears, suffering stops.
rows.riskNotes: The suggestion that pain ceases when one stops wishing to injure could feel blaming to those in shame, grief, or victimized by harm, implying their suffering is self-inflicted.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who avoids harmful actions, words, and thoughts, and maintains discipline in these three areas, is truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel like an impossible demand for perfect self-control, potentially deepening shame and self-blame in those already struggling with feelings of inadequacy or trauma.

rows.text: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When someone has grasped the Buddha's teaching, they should honor it with the same careful attention a priest gives to a ritual fire.
rows.riskNotes: The exhortation to worship the law carefully may feel like an additional burden or a standard of perfection that could intensify feelings of inadequacy in those experiencing shame, self-blame, despair, or suicidal ideation.

rows.text: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's worth is not determined by outward appearance, ancestry, or social standing, but by living truthfully and with integrity.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on inner truth and righteousness as the basis for being 'blessed' could deepen feelings of unworthiness in someone already struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Elaborate hairstyles and ascetic clothing are worthless if your mind is consumed by greed. You focus on cleaning the outside while ignoring the inner impurity.
rows.riskNotes: This harsh rebuke could deepen feelings of shame and self-loathing in someone already struggling with self-blame or despair.

rows.text: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who lives alone in the woods, wears worn-out clothes, is thin and veiny, and practices meditation is what I call an enlightened person.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of emaciation and isolation may be interpreted as endorsing self-neglect, which could be harmful to those experiencing self-blame, despair, or suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's true worth isn't determined by birth or wealth. The arrogant rich person is not necessarily noble; the one who has let go of all clinging, even if poor, is truly honorable.
rows.riskNotes: It may deepen shame or self-blame in those who identify with the 'arrogant wealthy' and feel invalidated, and could feel dismissive to those in despair or rage at systemic inequality.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has severed every mental chain, remains unshaken, and stands free and unattached.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may feel invalidating or discouraging to those struggling with emotional pain, trauma, or grief, as it presents an ideal of unshakeable freedom that can seem out of reach.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has cut through every kind of inner bondage and broken through all obstacles, and has awakened to the truth, is truly a noble person.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of cutting bonds can feel like a dismissal of real pain or an implicit demand to detach, which may deepen shame, grief, or hopelessness in those already struggling.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who, though innocent, patiently endures blame, imprisonment, and physical punishment, relying on endurance as their strength and inner resolve as their protection.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may pressure those who have suffered unjust harm to silently endure, potentially invalidating their pain and discouraging them from seeking justice or support.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has no anger, fulfills their duties, acts with integrity, has no cravings, is disciplined, and will not be reborn is a truly noble person.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being free from anger and subdued may shame those struggling with justified anger or self-blame, and could invalidate the pain of abuse survivors.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of genuine spiritual attainment does not grasp at pleasures; they slip away like water on a lotus leaf or a mustard seed on the point of a needle.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who fully understands the end of suffering, has laid down every burden, and is completely free from all attachments is truly a noble person.
rows.riskNotes: The description of ending suffering may feel like an unreachable ideal to those in deep pain, potentially intensifying feelings of inadequacy or hopelessness.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person is one who has profound understanding, clearly discerns right from wrong, and has reached the ultimate goal.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of knowing right and wrong and attaining the highest end may deepen feelings of inadequacy in those experiencing shame, self-blame, or despair, and the mention of 'highest end' could be misread as death by someone grieving.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A genuine holy person stays away from both laypeople and monks, avoids visiting households, and is content with very little.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's praise of aloofness and few desires may deepen isolation and self-blame in those already feeling disconnected or unworthy, and could discourage seeking support in times of grief or crisis.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person does not criticize any living being, whether weak or strong, and refrains from killing or causing others to kill.
rows.riskNotes: The call to find no fault with others can feel invalidating to those who have been harmed or are processing abuse, and the prohibition on killing may be distressing for someone experiencing suicidal thoughts.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person remains patient with those who are hostile, gentle with those who criticize, and calm when surrounded by strong desires.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may pressure survivors of abuse or relational harm to tolerate mistreatment, potentially invalidating their pain.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has shed anger, resentment, arrogance, and jealousy so thoroughly that they fall away without effort is a genuine spiritual person.
rows.riskNotes: The image of effortlessly dropping anger and shame can feel like an unattainable ideal or a dismissal of legitimate pain for those in crisis or relational trauma.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly noble person speaks words that are truthful, helpful, and gentle, so as not to cause anyone offense.
rows.riskNotes: This passage may pressure survivors of harm to suppress their anger or avoid speaking out, potentially invalidating their need to express pain.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who never takes anything that hasn't been freely offered, no matter its size or value, is truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of not taking what is not given may intensify feelings of guilt or unworthiness in someone already struggling with shame or self-blame.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has no desires for this life or any afterlife, who is free from all attachments and mental bonds, is truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of desirelessness may feel invalidating to those grieving, struggling with self-blame, or experiencing suicidal thoughts, as it could be perceived as dismissing their pain or encouraging detachment from life.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who is free from clinging, has realized the truth without hesitation, and has reached the unconditioned state is worthy of respect.
rows.riskNotes: The idealization of having no interests and not questioning could invalidate those in grief, despair, or crisis, and may be misinterpreted as encouraging detachment from pain or silencing legitimate questions.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has gone beyond the categories of good and bad, who is no longer bound by either, and who is free from sorrow, wrongdoing, and inner defilement.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being free from grief and sin may feel invalidating or like an unattainable standard for someone in shame, self-blame, or fresh grief.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who is clear and calm like the moon, completely at peace and free from any kind of restless excitement, is a truly awakened person.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of extinguishing all gaiety can be misread as advocating a joyless state, which may deepen despair or suicidal thoughts, while the standard of purity and serenity could intensify shame or self-blame in vulnerable readers.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has crossed the treacherous swamp of worldly life and reached the far shore is reflective, sincere, free from doubt and attachment, and deeply satisfied.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of 'reaching the other shore' could be misinterpreted as an escape from suffering through death, potentially harmful for those with suicidal ideation; the description of a perfected state may deepen feelings of inadequacy in despair.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true holy person is one who has given up all desires, lives without a fixed home, and has completely ended all lust.
rows.riskNotes: The idealization of desirelessness and homelessness may feel invalidating or shaming to those struggling with attachment, grief, or trauma, and could be misinterpreted as endorsing self-annihilation.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has abandoned all desires, lives without a permanent home, and has completely eliminated greed is truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: This passage's idealization of desirelessness and homelessness could feel invalidating or shaming to those in grief, despair, or self-blame, and may be misinterpreted by someone with suicidal ideation as endorsing detachment from life.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has freed themselves from every form of dependency—whether on people or on higher powers—and is entirely unbound is a true sage.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of total freedom from bondage may feel dismissive or unattainable to those in despair, suicidal ideation, or trapped in abusive relationships, potentially deepening feelings of failure or isolation.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This describes a person who has let go of both enjoyment and suffering, remains emotionally cool and detached, has no seeds left for future rebirth, and has overcome every realm of existence.
rows.riskNotes: The idealization of being 'cold' and leaving all pain can feel dismissive to those in grief, despair, or suicidal states, and may be misinterpreted as endorsing emotional numbness or escape from life.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who understands the cycle of death and rebirth for all living things, who is no longer trapped by attachments, and who has reached a state of well-being and full awakening is truly noble.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of 'destruction and return' may distress those in fresh grief or suicidal ideation by invoking death and rebirth, and the idealized state could feel unattainable to someone in despair.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completely extinguished all inner passions and reached a state of enlightenment that even gods and spirits cannot comprehend.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of extinguishing passions may feel invalidating or shaming to those struggling with intense emotions, grief, or trauma.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of genuine spiritual maturity owns nothing, has no clinging to any time or thing, lives with little, and is released from craving for the world.
rows.riskNotes: Idealizing detachment and poverty may invalidate grief, shame, or anger, potentially deepening hopelessness for those in crisis.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This describes a person who has reached the highest spiritual attainment: they are noble, heroic, a great sage, a conqueror of inner defilements, unshakable, accomplished, and fully awakened.
rows.riskNotes: The idealized description of an enlightened being may deepen feelings of inadequacy and hopelessness in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who remembers past lives, sees the full range of existence, has ended the cycle of rebirth, and has perfected all spiritual qualities is truly awakened.
rows.riskNotes: The mention of heaven and hell and the ideal of perfect knowledge may intensify feelings of unworthiness or hopelessness in those struggling with shame, self-blame, or despair.
